Vary it to a 'small'-scale instance, variant #12, skinned as 'drone delivery swarm'. Anchor idea: Construct a 20x20 +/-1 matrix maximizing |det|/20^10; verify.py runs Bareiss exactly.

why_it_generalizes: Build a finite integer set optimizing a closed-form arithmetic objective (sumset/difference-set exponent |A+A|/|A-A|, minimum-overlap). Purely arithmetic exact scoring, cheap, parameterizable by set-size budget.
seed_example: Construct an integer set A of size <=200 maximizing |A+A|/|A-A|; verifier computes both sumsets exactly.
brief: [C|quality-metric] Instantiate a NOVEL, deterministically-scored problem in family 'additive-combinatorics-set' (tier A), inspired by AlphaEvolve,SimpleTES. Vary it to a 'small'-scale instance, variant #12, skinned as 'quantum lab wiring'. Anchor idea: Construct an integer set A of size <=200 maximizing |A+A|/|A-A|; verifier computes both sumsets exactly.

why_it_generalizes: Output a rank-R decomposition of a matrix-multiplication (or general small 3D) tensor; counter reconstructs the tensor (entries rounded to nearest integer/half-integer) for an EXACT-equality gate, then score = R (# scalar multiplications). Parameterize over <m,n,p> with m,n,p<=5 and field (real/complex) -> the offline-safe surrogate for kernel FLOP reduction.
seed_example: Find a rank-R decomposition of the <3,3,3> matmul tensor over the rationals; counter verifies exact reconstruction and reports R (lower is better).
brief: [D|flops] Instantiate a NOVEL, deterministically-scored problem in family 'tensor-decomposition-rank' (tier A), inspired by AlphaEvolve. Vary it to a 'small'-scale instance, variant #12, skinned as 'harbor container port'. Anchor idea: Find a rank-R decomposition of the <3,3,3> matmul tensor over the rationals; counter verifies exact reconstruction and reports R (lower is better).

why_it_generalizes: Emit a point set; score = a deterministically computed geometric measure (star discrepancy / spherical-code min angle). Same construct-then-exactly-measure archetype as circle packing, scalable in size and dimension.
seed_example: Emit M points in [0,1]^d minimizing star discrepancy; verifier computes the discrepancy exactly over the induced boxes.
brief: [C|quality-metric] Instantiate a NOVEL, deterministically-scored problem in family 'low-discrepancy-pointset' (tier A), inspired by OpenEvolve (star-discrepancy),AlphaEvolve. Vary it to a 'small'-scale instance, variant #12, skinned as 'tide pool ecology'. Anchor idea: Emit M points in [0,1]^d minimizing star discrepancy; verifier computes the discrepancy exactly over the induced boxes.

why_it_generalizes: AtCoder-heuristic-contest problems reframed as STATIC instances with a deterministic contest scorer under a FIXED iteration/op budget (not wall-time). Keeps the long-horizon objective-driven search while being reproducible offline; best-of-N/self-refine over the objective teaches iterative optimization.
seed_example: Given a fixed fishing-region instance, output a polygon selection scored by the official deterministic contest formula under a 1e7-operation budget enforced by the harness.
brief: [B|quality-metric] Instantiate a NOVEL, deterministically-scored problem in family 'heuristic-contest-offline' (tier A), inspired by ALE-Bench (AHC),TTT-Discover (ahc). Vary it to a 'small'-scale instance, variant #12, skinned as 'data-center cooling'. Anchor idea: Given a fixed fishing-region instance, output a polygon selection scored by the official deterministic contest formula under a 1e7-operation budget enforced by the harness.

why_it_generalizes: The single FunSearch template 'evolve priority(element, params); a frozen scaffold greedily builds a valid set; score = cardinality' covers cap sets, admissible sets, corner-free sets, and independent sets in graph products. Batch-generate by varying (n,w,num_nodes,power,constraint-family) so the model learns 'design a good ordering heuristic under a validity checker', not one construction.
seed_example: Emit a priority over F_3^n vectors; scaffold greedily builds a cap set (no 3 collinear); score = |set| for n=4..7 (small=fast reward, large=eval).
brief: [C|correctness] Instantiate a NOVEL, deterministically-scored problem in family 'greedy-priority-construction' (tier A), inspired by FunSearch (cap-set/admissible/corner-free/cyclic-graph). Vary it to a 'medium'-scale instance, variant #13, skinned as 'deep-sea cable network'. Anchor idea: Emit a priority over F_3^n vectors; scaffold greedily builds a cap set (no 3 collinear); score = |set| for n=4..7 (small=fast reward, large=eval).

why_it_generalizes: Model writes a scoring function consumed by a FIXED streaming simulator (bin-packing priority(item,bins) -> -avg bins). Generalize by sampling instance distributions (uniform/Weibull, varying capacity/item-count) and reporting % excess over the L1 lower bound so sizes are comparable -> teaches online-algorithm design.
seed_example: Evolve priority(item, bins) for online 1D bin packing; simulator places each arrival in the highest-priority valid bin; score = -mean(bins) as % over L1 bound across an instance family.
brief: [B|quality-metric] Instantiate a NOVEL, deterministically-scored problem in family 'online-heuristic-simulator' (tier A), inspired by FunSearch (bin-packing),ALE-Bench (dispatch rules). Vary it to a 'medium'-scale instance, variant #13, skinned as 'power grid'. Anchor idea: Evolve priority(item, bins) for online 1D bin packing; simulator places each arrival in the highest-priority valid bin; score = -mean(bins) as % over L1 bound across an instance family.

why_it_generalizes: Emit coordinates+radii; an O(N^2) verifier checks containment+non-overlap then scores a continuous objective (sum of radii / min outer size). Parameterize by N, container shape, dimension -> a large family teaching constraint-respecting numeric construction + refinement.
seed_example: Place N circles in the unit square maximizing sum of radii (N=10..40); verify.py checks 1e-6 non-overlap+containment, score=sum(r).
brief: [C|quality-metric] Instantiate a NOVEL, deterministically-scored problem in family 'geometric-packing' (tier A), inspired by AlphaEvolve,OpenEvolve. Vary it to a 'medium'-scale instance, variant #13, skinned as 'aquarium plumbing'. Anchor idea: Place N circles in the unit square maximizing sum of radii (N=10..40); verify.py checks 1e-6 non-overlap+containment, score=sum(r).

why_it_generalizes: Kissing-number integer point sets (max-norm < min pairwise distance), Heilbronn (maximize min triangle area), and min max/min pairwise-distance ratio all score deterministically from coordinates; parameterize by n and dimension d for many difficulty levels.
seed_example: Place 11 points in the unit triangle maximizing the minimum triangle area over all triples; verifier computes the min area exactly.
brief: [C|quality-metric] Instantiate a NOVEL, deterministically-scored problem in family 'extremal-point-config' (tier A), inspired by AlphaEvolve. Vary it to a 'medium'-scale instance, variant #13, skinned as 'quantum lab wiring'. Anchor idea: Place 11 points in the unit triangle maximizing the minimum triangle area over all triples; verifier computes the min area exactly.

why_it_generalizes: Represent an unknown function as an N-interval step function (or Hermite basis) and compute a bound via deterministic convolution/integration/root-finding. Covers autocorrelation C1/C2/C3, uncertainty, and Erdos minimum-overlap; vary N, which functional (L1/L2/Linf ratio), and min-vs-max direction.
seed_example: Emit a non-negative step sequence f; score c1 = 2n*max(conv(f,f))/sum(f)^2 to MINIMIZE (first autocorrelation inequality), vary n_steps.
brief: [C|quality-metric] Instantiate a NOVEL, deterministically-scored problem in family 'functional-inequality-constant' (tier A), inspired by AlphaEvolve,OpenEvolve. Vary it to a 'medium'-scale instance, variant #13, skinned as 'data-center cooling'. Anchor idea: Emit a non-negative step sequence f; score c1 = 2n*max(conv(f,f))/sum(f)^2 to MINIMIZE (first autocorrelation inequality), vary n_steps.

why_it_generalizes: Emit an NxN +/-1 (or integer) matrix; verifier checks entries then computes EXACT integer determinant via Bareiss and scores |det|/theoretical_max. Exact integer arithmetic (no float tolerance); scales cleanly with N and matrix family (Hadamard/max-det/orthogonality-defect).
seed_example: Construct a 20x20 +/-1 matrix maximizing |det|/20^10; verify.py runs Bareiss exactly.
brief: [C|quality-metric] Instantiate a NOVEL, deterministically-scored problem in family 'integer-matrix-construction' (tier A), inspired by OpenEvolve,ThetaEvolve. Vary it to a 'medium'-scale instance, variant #13, skinned as 'traffic signal grid'. Anchor idea: Construct a 20x20 +/-1 matrix maximizing |det|/20^10; verify.py runs Bareiss exactly.

why_it_generalizes: Maximize Pareto hypervolume under a FIXED evaluation budget on a deterministic surrogate; teaches budgeted multi-objective trade-off search, deterministic given seeds+budget.
seed_example: Propose a batch of query points on the DTLZ2 surface maximizing hypervolume under a 50-evaluation budget; evaluator computes hypervolume exactly.
brief: [B|quality-metric] Instantiate a NOVEL, deterministically-scored problem in family 'multiobjective-hypervolume' (tier B), inspired by Frontier-Eng (ReactionOptimisation/DTLZ2). Vary it to a 'small'-scale instance, variant #3, skinned as 'solar farm inverters'. Anchor idea: Propose a batch of query points on the DTLZ2 surface maximizing hypervolume under a 50-evaluation budget; evaluator computes hypervolume exactly.

why_it_generalizes: Deterministic simulated-cost policies over fixed traces/tables: spot-vs-on-demand scheduling, multi-cloud broadcast cost, cost-aware model routing, KV-cache column ordering. No timing; teaches offline sequential decision + closed-form cost minimization that generalizes to unseen traces.
seed_example: Reorder SQL predicate columns to maximize prefix KV-cache hit-rate; evaluator computes (hit_rate-baseline)/(1-baseline)*100 on fixed data (the 10s cap is only a gate).
brief: [B|quality-metric] Instantiate a NOVEL, deterministically-scored problem in family 'offline-decision-policy' (tier B), inspired by Frontier-CS (cant_be_late/cloudcast/llm_router/llm_sql). Vary it to a 'small'-scale instance, variant #3, skinned as 'greenhouse zones'. Anchor idea: Reorder SQL predicate columns to maximize prefix KV-cache hit-rate; evaluator computes (hit_rate-baseline)/(1-baseline)*100 on fixed data (the 10s cap is only a gate).

why_it_generalizes: Minimize truss/tower weight or beam compliance subject to hard FEM stress/displacement/volume constraints (objective if feasible else +inf). Pure-numpy FEM on CPU; teaches 'find the best design within budget under a hard feasibility gate' -> the generative-optimization thesis.
seed_example: Choose bar cross-sections for a 45-bar 2D truss minimizing weight subject to stress+displacement limits; evaluator runs the numpy FEM and returns weight-if-feasible.
brief: [B|quality-metric] Instantiate a NOVEL, deterministically-scored problem in family 'feasibility-gated-structural' (tier B), inspired by Frontier-Eng (StructuralOptimization). Vary it to a 'medium'-scale instance, variant #4, skinned as 'aquarium plumbing'. Anchor idea: Choose bar cross-sections for a 45-bar 2D truss minimizing weight subject to stress+displacement limits; evaluator runs the numpy FEM and returns weight-if-feasible.

why_it_generalizes: Inventory (MEIO/joint-replenishment/EOQ) and portfolio MIP/CVaR scored by deterministic composite objectives normalized against baseline+reference; numpy/cvxpy CPU. Teaches constrained mixed-integer/convex trade-off search.
seed_example: Set safety-stock levels across a supply tree minimizing holding+shortage cost under a service-level constraint; evaluator computes the composite objective normalized to 100.
brief: [B|quality-metric] Instantiate a NOVEL, deterministically-scored problem in family 'constrained-OR' (tier B), inspired by Frontier-Eng (Inventory/Portfolio). Vary it to a 'medium'-scale instance, variant #4, skinned as 'coral reef survey'. Anchor idea: Set safety-stock levels across a supply tree minimizing holding+shortage cost under a service-level constraint; evaluator computes the composite objective normalized to 100.

why_it_generalizes: Adaptive-optics/holography efficiency & uniformity and importance-sampling BER/variance-reduction are deterministic-per-seed numpy objectives -> teaches iterative refinement on a continuous simulated reward without any hardware.
seed_example: Design a phase mask maximizing diffraction efficiency + uniformity of a target spot array; evaluator simulates propagation with a fixed seed and returns the composite metric.
brief: [B|quality-metric] Instantiate a NOVEL, deterministically-scored problem in family 'seeded-numerical-sim' (tier B), inspired by Frontier-Eng (Optics/Communications). Vary it to a 'medium'-scale instance, variant #4, skinned as 'forest fire watchtowers'. Anchor idea: Design a phase mask maximizing diffraction efficiency + uniformity of a target spot array; evaluator simulates propagation with a fixed seed and returns the composite metric.

why_it_generalizes: Minimize gate/SWAP count and depth for QFT/QAOA/Clifford+T circuits on a named backend, verified by functional equivalence. A clean deterministic count-based reward -> the offline surrogate capturing kernel-like 'make the artifact cheaper' without timing.
seed_example: Emit a routing/transpilation for a QAOA circuit on the IBM Falcon coupling map minimizing added SWAP gates; counter checks equivalence and returns the SWAP count.
brief: [D|flops] Instantiate a NOVEL, deterministically-scored problem in family 'quantum-circuit-opcount' (tier B), inspired by Frontier-Eng (QuantumComputing),SimpleTES. Vary it to a 'medium'-scale instance, variant #4, skinned as 'vaccine cold chain'. Anchor idea: Emit a routing/transpilation for a QAOA circuit on the IBM Falcon coupling map minimizing added SWAP gates; counter checks equivalence and returns the SWAP count.

why_it_generalizes: Fit a closed-form f(x) minimizing held-out MSE with an expression-complexity penalty. The train/held-out split forces the model to search expression space and GENERALIZE, not memorize points -> pure NumPy/SymPy deterministic score.
seed_example: Fit a closed-form expression to sampled points from a hidden 4-D poly-exp formula; score = f(heldout R^2, complexity).
brief: [E|quality-metric] Instantiate a NOVEL, deterministically-scored problem in family 'symbolic-regression' (tier B), inspired by Frontier-CS (symbolic_regression),OpenEvolve. Vary it to a 'medium'-scale instance, variant #4, skinned as 'archaeology dig grid'. Anchor idea: Fit a closed-form expression to sampled points from a hidden 4-D poly-exp formula; score = f(heldout R^2, complexity).

why_it_generalizes: Derive a scaling-law functional form and fit it, scored by EXTRAPOLATION error on a held-out region. Deterministic CPU regression; the natural train/unseen axis directly measures generalization vs overfit.
seed_example: Given a (compute, loss) table, propose+fit a parametric scaling law; score = extrapolation error at held-out larger-compute points.
brief: [E|quality-metric] Instantiate a NOVEL, deterministically-scored problem in family 'scientific-law-extrapolation' (tier B), inspired by SimpleTES (scaling laws). Vary it to a 'medium'-scale instance, variant #4, skinned as 'rooftop gardens'. Anchor idea: Given a (compute, loss) table, propose+fit a parametric scaling law; score = extrapolation error at held-out larger-compute points.

brief: [D|flops] Instantiate a NOVEL, deterministically-scored problem in family 'quantum-circuit-opcount' (tier B), inspired by Frontier-Eng (QuantumComputing),SimpleTES. Vary it to a 'small'-scale instance, variant #6, skinned as 'reservoir dam network'. Anchor idea: Emit a routing/transpilation for a QAOA circuit on the IBM Falcon coupling map minimizing added SWAP gates; counter checks equivalence and returns the SWAP count.

why_it_generalizes: Fit a closed-form f(x) minimizing held-out MSE with an expression-complexity penalty. The train/held-out split forces the model to search expression space and GENERALIZE, not memorize points -> pure NumPy/SymPy deterministic score.
seed_example: Fit a closed-form expression to sampled points from a hidden 4-D poly-exp formula; score = f(heldout R^2, complexity).
brief: [E|quality-metric] Instantiate a NOVEL, deterministically-scored problem in family 'symbolic-regression' (tier B), inspired by Frontier-CS (symbolic_regression),OpenEvolve. Vary it to a 'small'-scale instance, variant #6, skinned as 'bakery supply chain'. Anchor idea: Fit a closed-form expression to sampled points from a hidden 4-D poly-exp formula; score = f(heldout R^2, complexity).

why_it_generalizes: Derive a scaling-law functional form and fit it, scored by EXTRAPOLATION error on a held-out region. Deterministic CPU regression; the natural train/unseen axis directly measures generalization vs overfit.
seed_example: Given a (compute, loss) table, propose+fit a parametric scaling law; score = extrapolation error at held-out larger-compute points.
brief: [E|quality-metric] Instantiate a NOVEL, deterministically-scored problem in family 'scientific-law-extrapolation' (tier B), inspired by SimpleTES (scaling laws). Vary it to a 'small'-scale instance, variant #6, skinned as 'wind tunnel sensors'. Anchor idea: Given a (compute, loss) table, propose+fit a parametric scaling law; score = extrapolation error at held-out larger-compute points.

why_it_generalizes: Maximize Pareto hypervolume under a FIXED evaluation budget on a deterministic surrogate; teaches budgeted multi-objective trade-off search, deterministic given seeds+budget.
seed_example: Propose a batch of query points on the DTLZ2 surface maximizing hypervolume under a 50-evaluation budget; evaluator computes hypervolume exactly.
brief: [B|quality-metric] Instantiate a NOVEL, deterministically-scored problem in family 'multiobjective-hypervolume' (tier B), inspired by Frontier-Eng (ReactionOptimisation/DTLZ2). Vary it to a 'small'-scale instance, variant #6, skinned as 'harbor container port'. Anchor idea: Propose a batch of query points on the DTLZ2 surface maximizing hypervolume under a 50-evaluation budget; evaluator computes hypervolume exactly.

why_it_generalizes: Deterministic simulated-cost policies over fixed traces/tables: spot-vs-on-demand scheduling, multi-cloud broadcast cost, cost-aware model routing, KV-cache column ordering. No timing; teaches offline sequential decision + closed-form cost minimization that generalizes to unseen traces.
seed_example: Reorder SQL predicate columns to maximize prefix KV-cache hit-rate; evaluator computes (hit_rate-baseline)/(1-baseline)*100 on fixed data (the 10s cap is only a gate).
brief: [B|quality-metric] Instantiate a NOVEL, deterministically-scored problem in family 'offline-decision-policy' (tier B), inspired by Frontier-CS (cant_be_late/cloudcast/llm_router/llm_sql). Anchor idea: Reorder SQL predicate columns to maximize prefix KV-cache hit-rate; evaluator computes (hit_rate-baseline)/(1-baseline)*100 on fixed data (the 10s cap is only a gate).

why_it_generalizes: composite objective + mathematical twist forces multi-mechanism reasoning
seed_example: Remove <=k links (budget) so that EVERY surviving path from source s to sink t has length that is COMPOSITE (never prime); minimize (removal cost + a penalty per surviving prime-length s-t path). Composite: interdiction budget x number-theoretic path-length constraint. gen must plant instances where the greedy 'cut the shortest path' is a trap.
brief: [A|BESPOKE-NOVELTY] Author a NOVEL, DEEP, deterministically-scored testlib problem in family 'graph-interdiction-primality', objective=minimize, skin 'quantum lab wiring'. This must be a TOP-tier bespoke problem, NOT a textbook NP-hard core in a skin. Core idea: Remove <=k links (budget) so that EVERY surviving path from source s to sink t has length that is COMPOSITE (never prime); minimize (removal cost + a penalty per surviving prime-length s-t path). Composite: interdiction budget x number-theoretic path-length constraint. gen must plant instances where the greedy 'cut the shortest path' is a trap. Follow the NOVELTY section of the brief (composite objective, the math twist, non-linear coupled scoring, and an ADVERSARIAL generator with planted/trap/needle cases that FILLS the stated constraint envelope on the largest tests).

seed_example: Over T rounds, each round pick sensors (paying per activation); coverage a sensor gives DECAYS each round it stays on and CARRIES OVER when off. Minimize cost + outage/resonance penalties. Trap: first N-1 cheapest fallback links are connected but resonate; planted modules and needle stations reward extra timed links.
theme: emergency radio relays with time-windowed interference and modular resonance
brief: [A|BESPOKE-NOVELTY] Author a NOVEL, DEEP, deterministically-scored testlib problem in family 'temporal-network-modular-resilience', objective=minimize, skin 'emergency radio relays with time-windowed interference and modular resonance'. The cheapest fallback tree is a trap, and the generator includes planted modules, traps, needles, and large envelope-filling cases.

why_it_generalizes: composite facility placement, geometric reach, explicit routed flow, and convex congestion force balanced construction rather than plain set cover
seed_example: Open flood-barrier pumps on a canal grid; every basin must be assigned to an opened pump within Manhattan reach and routed along canal cells. Minimize opening cost + routed distance + squared geometric distance + convex edge-load congestion. Trap: cheap high-radius central pumps cover everything but overload narrow canals; strong solutions spread district pumps and reroute through wider relief canals.
theme: flood-barrier pump placement on a canal grid
brief: [A|BESPOKE-NOVELTY] Author a NOVEL, DEEP, deterministically-scored testlib problem in family 'geometric-flow-congestion-coverage', objective=minimize, skin 'flood-barrier pump placement on a canal grid'. Core idea: Open pumps, assign every basin to an opened pump within Manhattan reach, and output explicit canal routes. Score combines fixed opening cost, geometric distance, route length, and convex congestion on low-width canal edges. The obvious cover-every-basin greedily strategy overloads narrow canals; stronger solutions spread pumps and reroute flow. Include planted/trap/needle generator cases and a real solution ladder.

innovation_hook: Conduction is a series circuit: the score is set by the worst bottleneck on the source-to-boundary path, so budget belongs in corridors, not blankets.
why_it_generalizes: Trains recognizing that local greedy credit assignment fails when the objective is governed by a global transport path (min-cut/series-resistance reasoning), a pattern recurring in routing, networking, and thermal design.
seed_example: gen.py emits an HxW grid (<=200x200) with integer-wattage heat sources and cold ambient boundary cells; the solver prints K coordinates of high-conductivity cells. verify.py runs a fixed 5000-iteration integer diffusion relaxation and reports the maximum steady-state temperature (minimize). Trap: greedily blanketing the hottest sources with conductive cells barely helps because the heat still has nowhere to go; strong solutions spend most of the budget on contiguous corridors from sources to the cold boundary, far from any source.

innovation_hook: Because settled material is permanent and later pours flow over it, you must plan for downhill runoff of future stages rather than greedily matching the target now.
why_it_generalizes: Trains anticipating how an irreversible physical relaxation composes across stages — a transferable habit of reasoning about monotone accumulation with propagation.
seed_example: Input: an N-cell target height profile and K pour stages; the solver outputs for each stage a cell index and an integer grain count. After each pour the pile relaxes deterministically by an abelian angle-of-repose rule (any cell exceeding its neighbor by >slope topples one grain downhill until stable). The checker replays all K pours in order and scores the L1 distance between the final settled profile and the target. Trap: matching the target cell-by-cell each stage over-pours, because grains from later pours flow downhill over earlier deposits and nothing can ever be removed — you must under-pour early and reserve valleys for the runoff of stage K.

innovation_hook: Kerf turns packing feasibility into a function of the cut tree's shape, so the blade budget must be rationed by marginal kerf saved per piece, not by cut order.
why_it_generalizes: Resource-annotated divide-and-conquer trees where the recursion itself consumes the resource appear in scheduling, memory tiling, and query planning.
seed_example: Input: sheet W x H, demanded rectangles with values, and two blades: kerf 3.0mm with unlimited use, kerf 0.4mm with a total cut-length wear budget L. Solver outputs a guillotine cut tree where every cut names its blade; the checker replays the tree, physically subtracting each cut's kerf from the geometry, verifies each demanded piece is realized at exact size, and scores total value extracted. Trap: nesting with zero-kerf arithmetic and deriving cuts afterwards fails because kerf loss depends on cut-tree topology (deep trees eat more material), and spending the thin blade greedily on the first long cuts exhausts L before the small high-value pieces that actually need it.

why_it_generalizes: Teaches decomposing a global combinatorial objective through an algebraic product structure, a reusable move for any design problem over composite moduli.

innovation_hook: Forbidden factors turn a clean Eulerian cover into a max-edge rural-postman walk on a mutilated de Bruijn graph.
why_it_generalizes: Rewards reformulating coverage as constrained graph traversal rather than pattern spamming, a transferable move across covering problems.
seed_example: Input: alphabet size a, factor length k, and a seeded forbidden set F of length-k strings. The solver outputs one string S; the checker verifies S contains no forbidden factor and counts how many distinct ALLOWED length-k strings occur as factors, scoring coverage/total with a length tiebreak. Trap: emitting a de Bruijn sequence via an Eulerian circuit ignores F and gets disqualified, while naive greedy extension dead-ends early. The insight is to delete forbidden edges from the (k-1)-mer de Bruijn graph and find a walk covering the maximum remaining edges despite broken Eulerity.

innovation_hook: Capacities are not protection, they are routing: the winning design deliberately under-provisions chosen lines so every cascade in the sweep dies in a low-value pocket instead of crossing the backbone.
why_it_generalizes: Teaches reasoning about second-order dynamics (how a defense changes the failure trajectory) rather than first-order margins, a pattern shared by all cascading-failure and systemic-risk design.
seed_example: Input: a flow network (n<=2000 lines) with base load per line and a total capacity budget B; solver outputs one capacity per line summing to <=B. The checker trips each of 50 designated lines, redistributes load by a fixed proportional rule, trips any over-capacity line, iterates to a fixpoint, and scores min over the 50 scenarios of served-demand fraction. Trap: sizing capacity proportional to base load leaves the same corridor lines as cascade amplifiers in every scenario; the ceiling requires shaping capacities so cascades are steered into sacrificial low-demand pockets.

innovation_hook: Equilibrium latency is non-monotone in the edge set, so deleting a tempting shortcut can strictly lower selfish-routing cost.
why_it_generalizes: Teaches that adding capacity or options can hurt under equilibrium, a structural counter-intuition transferable to any congestion or contention design.
seed_example: Input: a directed graph with per-edge latency ell_e(x)=a_e+b_e*x^k (k>=2) and fixed s-t demand D. Solver outputs a subset of edges to KEEP. Checker computes the Wardrop selfish-routing equilibrium flow on the kept subnetwork by deterministic Frank-Wolfe to a fixed duality gap and reports total travel time (minimize). Trap: keeping every edge looks safe, but shortcut edges create Braess congestion, so the greedy 'use the whole network' solution sits far above the achievable equilibrium latency.
theme: Toll-free city removes roads to cut jams

innovation_hook: Fixing the top Cheeger cut is a mirage — the planted cut hierarchy means marginal lambda_2 return migrates between scales, so the winning move is multi-scale budget allocation guided by successive Fiedler vectors, not one-shot cut repair.
why_it_generalizes: Teaches that a global spectral quantity responds to local edits through a shifting bottleneck structure — the core skill of iterated diagnosis-then-edit rather than one static heuristic.
seed_example: Input: a weighted graph (n<=3000) generated with a hidden hierarchy of nested bottleneck cuts, plus a budget B; adding edge (u,v) costs hop-distance(u,v) in the original graph. Output: a list of new edges with total cost <= B. Checker recomputes the Laplacian and scores lambda_2 via deterministic inverse power iteration. Trap: dumping the whole budget on the single widest visible cut only exposes the next nested cut one level down; strong solutions price lambda_2-gain per cost unit and split the budget across scales using many cheap mid-range edges.

innovation_hook: Non-monotone price paths (engineered temporary spikes and V-shapes) act as a screening device separating patient from impatient buyers, so the optimum lies far from every monotone markdown template.
why_it_generalizes: Trains the core skill of the lens: your decision variable is transformed by an embedded best-response before scoring, so you must optimize the induced behavior, not the schedule itself -- a pattern spanning pricing, release timing, and staged commitments.
seed_example: Input: N buyers, each with value v_i, patience horizon h_i, per-day discount factor d_i, plus stock K; all printed. Solver outputs a price p_t for each of 30 days. verify.py replays buyers in fixed order: each buys on the day t<=h_i maximizing d_i^t*(v_i-p_t) among days with stock left, if positive; score = revenue. Trap: any monotone markdown ladder teaches every patient buyer to wait for the floor, collapsing revenue -- the generator plants a patient/impatient mixture so non-monotone paths dominate.

innovation_hook: The image is not data to copy but the fixpoint of an unknown recursion, so compression = discovering the generative rule, not tracing the outline.
why_it_generalizes: Rewards seeing structure as a program (minimum description length), the core scientist move of replacing enumeration with mechanism.
seed_example: Input: a target 2D 'organism' bitmap (128x128) produced by a hidden L-system. Solver outputs a bracketed L-system (axiom + rewrite rules + turtle interpretation + iteration count); verify.py expands it deterministically, rasterizes, requires >=99% pixel match, then scores total description length in tokens. Trap: emitting a literal move-by-move turtle path reproduces the image but is enormous, and the ceiling needs discovering the recursive rule whose fixpoint IS the shape, so recombining textbook curve primitives plateaus far above minimum length.

innovation_hook: The instance is a multivariate factorization problem in disguise: evaluation cost is a proxy for recovering the generative structure, not for clever term rewriting.
why_it_generalizes: Rewards reverse-engineering how surface data was generated before optimizing on it — the core scientist move of hypothesizing latent structure.
seed_example: gen.py emits k~20 sparse multivariate polynomials in fully expanded coefficient form; secretly each is a sum/product of a few hidden sparse factors, and factors are shared across the k polynomials. The solver submits a straight-line program (const/add/mul lines) computing all k values; counter.py verifies agreement on seeded evaluation points over F_p and scores exact multiplication count. Trap: Horner/CSE on the expanded forms costs thousands of mults, while recovering the shared factorization costs tens.

innovation_hook: In the slow regime a path-dependent latch is observationally indistinguishable from a static curve; the tell is that residuals correlate with drive DIRECTION, not drive VALUE, which forces you to posit hidden state.
why_it_generalizes: Detecting hidden state behind apparently memoryless data is the core move of system identification, RL environment modeling, and debugging stateful code.
seed_example: gen.py emits noisy (drive_temperature[t], heater_output[t]) traces produced by a latch with a hidden hysteresis band [L,H] plus a fixed k-step actuation lag, sampled only under SLOW monotone-ish drives; the solver reads the traces and prints a stateful predictor in a tiny DSL (comparisons, one latch register, delay taps). verify.py rolls the DSL program on held-out FAST non-monotone drives with minor loops and scores rollout MSE plus a per-DSL-node parsimony penalty. Trap: a memoryless sigmoid/threshold fit is near-perfect on the slow quasi-static training branch but is wrong on every direction reversal inside the band and misses the lag entirely.

innovation_hook: Because cooling is free but exponentially slowing, the optimal sequence is not temperature-monotone: it inserts reheat spikes exactly where the cooling tail is flatter than the deadline pressure.
why_it_generalizes: Paid-versus-free asymmetric state dynamics underlie ovens, chillers, ramp-up costs, and hysteresis resources everywhere, and force reasoning about state trajectories instead of static orderings.
seed_example: gen.cpp emits N<=5000 jobs, each with a firing temperature window [lo_i,hi_i], duration d_i, and deadline, plus kiln parameters (heating cost per degree, exponential free-cooling constant). The solver prints a job permutation with per-step heat/idle actions; chk.cc simulates the temperature trajectory exactly and emits Ratio from total heating energy plus lateness penalties. Trap: sorting hottest-first to ride the cooling curve ignores that cooling slows exponentially near ambient, so idling down to a cool job's window blows deadlines that a deliberate out-of-order reheat spike would meet cheaply.

innovation_hook: The right depots sit on 'hedge' points that trade a little cost in every season rather than minimizing pooled demand — you must cluster the SCENARIOS by geometry first, then site against the induced worst-case envelope.
why_it_generalizes: Worst-case siting against a scenario ensemble is the core of robust facility/CDN/warehouse placement; the average-vs-envelope trap recurs in every stochastic-programming surrogate.
seed_example: gen.cpp emits N=2000 city points and K=8 seasonal demand weight vectors; the solver prints P=15 depot coordinates fixed for all seasons; chk.cc computes weighted nearest-depot assignment cost per season and scores the MAXIMUM over seasons. The generator plants two seasons with nearly disjoint hot zones, so k-means on pooled (average) demand puts depots in dead middle ground and the worst season blows up.

innovation_hook: Energy-optimal solutions deliberately DETOUR to merge into shared corridors and rotate the lead position, so the real problem is convoy formation and leadership scheduling, not routing.
why_it_generalizes: Teaches that agent coupling can be a resource to engineer rather than a constraint to avoid — the same inversion appears in cache sharing, batched inference, and cooperative transport.
seed_example: Input: a weighted street grid, K couriers each with a start, a delivery target, and a deadline; moving an edge costs base energy, but costs 40% if you enter an edge on the same tick as another courier who traversed it the tick before (drafting), while leading a convoy adds +10%. Output: one timed route per courier (list of (tick, node)). The checker replays all routes, verifies moves are legal and deadlines met (heavy penalty otherwise), and sums exact energy. Trap: independent shortest paths per courier never overlap in time, so the obvious solution pays full energy everywhere and sits ~35% above the ceiling.

innovation_hook: Optimal fault-count and optimal dollar-cost diverge: you must schedule cheap proactive cleans during idle loop phases so future evictions are all clean, a two-timescale plan no single-metric eviction rule finds.
why_it_generalizes: Teaches separating the feasibility objective (what to keep) from the cost objective (when to pay), a pattern recurring in any system with asymmetric state-transition costs.
seed_example: Input: cache size k and a fixed trace of ~200k read/write ops over N pages containing cyclic sweeps of length k+2 interleaved with hot singletons; writes mark pages dirty. Solver outputs, for every miss, which resident page to evict plus optional proactive clean-writes; checker replays the trace, validates residency, and charges fetch=5, clean-evict=1, dirty-evict=12, proactive-clean=3. Trap: Belady's farthest-in-future minimizes faults but keeps evicting dirty pages at 12x cost; LRU thrashes on the k+2 loops. The score is total charged cost.

innovation_hook: Reasoning in optical-admittance space linearizes a reflectance objective that is hopelessly nonlinear layer-by-layer.
why_it_generalizes: Teaches inversion of a compositional matrix product by finding the space where composition becomes additive — a pattern reused across cascaded linear systems.

Trap: greedily adding a quarter-wave layer to null reflectance at each wavelength independently ignores that every layer couples all wavelengths at once, so the stack never converges to the band.

innovation_hook: A boolean permutation's cheap part is exactly its GF(2)-affine component; separate it first and the Toffoli cost collapses to the nonlinear residue.
why_it_generalizes: Teaches decomposing a hard object into a cheap linear shell plus a small irreducible core — a reusable 'peel the easy structure' move.
seed_example: Input = a permutation on {0,1}^n given by its action on all 2^n basis states (n<=12) plus a small ancilla budget a. The solver outputs a straight-line list of reversible gates (NOT, CNOT, Toffoli) over n+a wires; the checker simulates it, confirms it realizes the permutation exactly, and scores total gate count (lower better). Trap: synthesizing one Toffoli cascade per output minterm gives O(2^n) gates; the insight is to peel off the GF(2)-affine part with cheap CNOT/NOT layers so Toffolis are paid only for the small nonlinear residue.

innovation_hook: Gate acceptance on a running estimate of the remaining-capacity shadow price rather than on observed values, so a detected regime shift raises the bar and reserves capacity before the scarce high-value class arrives.
why_it_generalizes: Reservation pricing under nonstationary arrivals recurs across revenue management, scheduling, and online matching — the transferable skill is anticipating future value instead of fitting the past.
seed_example: Instances are streams of capacity requests, each tagged with a class and a value drawn from a per-class distribution that jumps at hidden mid-stream breakpoints; capacity is fixed. The program reads requests one at a time and outputs accept/reject, and the evaluator sums accepted value against an offline fractional optimum on the hidden values. On 3 of 10 instances the high-value class only appears after a regime shift, so an acceptance threshold fit to the early stream rejects exactly the requests that matter.

innovation_hook: Shed proactively along the predicted cascade frontier rather than at the present hotspot, keeping every redistribution below neighbor margins.
why_it_generalizes: Teaches reasoning about action-induced load redistribution and multi-step failure propagation, a skill reused across power, network, and supply-chain resilience where local relief creates downstream stress.
seed_example: Instances are a seeded sequence of line trips on a transmission grid; each step the program sees current line loads and margins and outputs which loads to shed and how much power to reroute, after which the transition deterministically pushes the shed/rerouted power onto neighbors and trips any line whose margin is exceeded, while unmet demand accumulates into a growing backlog. The evaluator sums served demand minus a convex backlog penalty over the whole episode across seeds and normalizes to [0,1]. The trap: a myopic policy that relieves the currently-most-overloaded line reroutes power onto a fragile corridor that cascades two steps later.

innovation_hook: Maximum load is a min-cut-like property of force paths: sparse, correctly triangulated load paths beat dense meshes, so quality comes from topology, not tonnage.
why_it_generalizes: Teaches that feasibility under a physics check is structural (rigidity/triangulation invariants), rewarding models that discover hidden validity conditions rather than pattern-matching 'more edges = stronger'.

Trap: a dense direct span wastes budget on redundant members, and untriangulated quadrilaterals hinge and score near zero under the balance check even though they 'look connected'.

innovation_hook: Spending scarce early budget on a distant choke point that only pays off once the fire arrives beats defending the current frontier.
why_it_generalizes: Encodes value-of-early-commitment against a spreading deterministic process, a pattern common to containment, quarantine, and cut-set reasoning.
seed_example: gen.cpp emits a weighted graph, ignition sources, and a per-timestep protection budget B. Fire spreads deterministically to all unprotected neighbors each timestep; the solver outputs, per timestep, which B vertices to permanently protect (protection is irreversible and blocks spread). chk.cc simulates to quiescence and reports total saved vertex weight. Trap: protecting the vertices nearest the flame front greedily wastes budget; the win is cutting a narrow cut-set choke several steps early to shield a large distant component before the fire can reach it.

why_it_generalizes: Order-dependent cost accumulation under a commutative-looking operation set is the core of build-order, matrix-chain, and merge-scheduling problems.

Trap: any sequence reaching the target looks fine, but greedy 'fold biggest dimension first' builds thick stacks early and every later crease carries them; interleaving axes and folding thin margins first keeps carried thickness logarithmic instead of linear.

innovation_hook: Sparsity is invariant under affine conjugation, so the right move is to search the 2-parameter (a,b) family and test whether the pulled-back data admits a t-term fit — turning an impossible extrapolation into a tiny algebraic search.
why_it_generalizes: Rewards recognizing that a hidden change of variables preserves an algebraic complexity measure, the core of symmetry-aware model discovery.
seed_example: gen.py fixes a t-sparse polynomial S over F_p and a hidden affine map u=ax+b, sets f(x)=S(ax+b), and reveals (x,f(x)) pairs only for x in a narrow training window; verify.py scores exact matches on held-out x drawn from the rest of F_p. Trap: dense Lagrange interpolation on the window fits training perfectly but is a degree-(window-1) monster that fails every held-out point. Solver must output a program/formula evaluated by verify.py.

innovation_hook: Exploiting the automaton's hidden group structure beats generic pairwise merging on synchronizing-word length.
why_it_generalizes: Teaches spotting algebraic invariants inside a transition system instead of running the textbook heuristic.
seed_example: Input: a seeded synchronizing DFA on n states over m symbols (transition table), built with planted cyclic+contraction (Cerny-family) structure. The solver outputs an input word; the checker simulates it from all states, verifies collapse to one state, and scores negative word length. Trap: greedy pairwise-collapse (repeatedly merge two active states with a shortest word) is polynomial but lands far above optimum on these instances, and subset-BFS explodes at 2^n. The insight is to read the planted permutation/contraction algebra and build a near-quadratically-shorter reset word.

innovation_hook: The sweep's ignition set is a lattice, so worst-case defense is a graph-partition problem (equalize compartment fuel) rather than a coverage problem around hot spots.
why_it_generalizes: Worst-case containment via balanced partitioning transfers to epidemics, malware segmentation, and blast-radius design in distributed systems.
seed_example: Input: a 300x300 fuel grid and a budget of F firebreak cells; solver prints the firebreak cell set. verify.py ignites at each of 64 fixed grid points, spreads fire deterministically to 4-neighbors with fuel unless blocked, and scores min saved fuel over the 64 ignitions. Trap: rings around high-fuel regions defend the average ignition but the sweep's corner ignitions flank them; the 64 points share a coarse lattice, so one balanced compartmentalization of the whole map beats any per-ignition defense.

innovation_hook: The socially optimal toll equals the marginal congestion externality (flow times marginal latency), not a level proportional to congestion.
why_it_generalizes: Marginal-cost internalization is a general mechanism-design principle reused across pricing, scheduling, and resource-contention policy.
seed_example: The submitted PROGRAM reads a road network with superlinear latencies and an O-D demand vector and outputs a toll per edge. The frozen evaluator adds tolls to latencies, computes the resulting user equilibrium over a fixed distribution of demand instances, and scores the average true toll-free total latency (minimize). Trap: tolls proportional to observed congestion push flow around wrongly and can raise latency; the optimum requires the marginal-externality toll equal to flow times marginal latency.

innovation_hook: Effective resistances cannot be tuned independently — the monotone global coupling forces solvers to reason about the whole pseudoinverse, and the best designs deliberately exploit shared edges to hit many targets at once.
why_it_generalizes: Inverse design under a globally coupled, monotone response is ubiquitous (networks, materials, markets); it punishes decomposition-into-subgoals and rewards a global model of interaction.
seed_example: Input: n terminals and P specified pairs each with a target effective resistance, plus an edge budget m and a weight cap. Output: a weighted graph on the n terminals. verify.py computes all specified effective resistances from the Laplacian pseudoinverse and scores weighted RMS error against the targets. Trap: building an independent series path per pair — by Rayleigh monotonicity every edge added for one pair lowers resistance for all others, so pair-by-pair fitting systematically overshoots; good solutions design one global current-flow skeleton (tree backbone plus selective parallel reinforcement).

innovation_hook: The best toll vector performs network surgery -- prohibitively tolling planted paradox edges (effectively deleting them) strictly beats any smooth congestion-proportional pricing rule.
why_it_generalizes: Forces reasoning about equilibrium counterfactuals rather than local marginal costs: interventions that look punitive locally can be globally beneficial, a recurring insight in routing, load balancing, and traffic shaping.
seed_example: gen.cpp emits a directed graph (n up to 2e4) with integer congestion-latency tables per edge and OD commuter demands. Submission prints an integer toll per edge under a total-toll-mass budget; chk.cc runs a fixed deterministic best-response cycle (commuters reroute in fixed order for 50 rounds) and scores total travel time, emitting Ratio. Trap: tolling the currently busiest edges pushes flow onto planted Braess-style shortcut gadgets and makes total time worse; the generator guarantees several such gadgets per instance.

innovation_hook: With a bounded sparse seed, the pattern must be produced by the rule's dynamics, not memorized in the initial state.
why_it_generalizes: Teaches designing dynamics that do computational work, separating what is stored from what is generated.
seed_example: gen.cpp emits a target binary image and a step count T. Solver outputs an initial cell configuration plus a 2D outer-totalistic CA rule table; chk.cc runs the CA for T steps and reports pixel overlap with the target as Ratio. Trap: encoding the target directly into a maximal dense seed scores high, but the generator caps seed density and T, so a hand-drawn seed cannot reach the target and you must find a rule whose dynamics self-organize the pattern from a sparse seed.

innovation_hook: The don't-care set is not noise to be completed but the main resource — detecting the affine geometry of the care set collapses the function.
why_it_generalizes: Teaches exploiting specification slack and detecting hidden low-dimensional structure in data supports.
seed_example: gen.py gives a partial truth table on n=14 inputs: ~4000 care vectors with required outputs; behavior elsewhere is free. Solver outputs a gate list (AND/OR/XOR/NOT); counter.py checks every care vector and scores gate count. Planted: the care set lies inside a low-dimensional affine subspace on which the target restricts to a low-degree function. Trap: completing to a total function and running Shannon/espresso-style synthesis yields a circuit 20x larger than one that exploits the freedom off the care set.

innovation_hook: Model the invariants of the map instead of the map — conservation laws are regime-independent and collapse an infinite hypothesis space to a one-bit branch decision.
why_it_generalizes: Searching for conserved quantities before fitting dynamics is the physicist's master trick and transfers to any structured-prediction problem with symmetry.
seed_example: gen.py provides noisy (pre, post) velocity pairs from two-body collisions that exactly conserve momentum and one unusual anisotropic quadratic form, but only at LOW collision energies; the solver outputs symbolic invariant expressions plus a post-state predictor. verify.py scores post-state prediction error on held-out HIGH-energy collisions plus symbol-count parsimony. Trap: directly regressing post=f(pre) on the training range interpolates beautifully and explodes at 10x energy, while the two invariants plus a discrete branch choice pin the map at every energy.

innovation_hook: Some jobs are disguised repair actions, so the real problem is jointly scheduling production and hidden maintenance rather than inserting resets into a fixed sequence.
why_it_generalizes: Recognizing that ordinary actions can double as state repair (self-healing workloads, cache-warming requests) transfers to any system where work and upkeep share one timeline.
seed_example: gen.py emits jobs with hardness h_i and size s_i; processing time is s_i*(1+wear)^2, wear increases by h_i*s_i, but jobs with negative h_i (soft dressing materials) REDUCE wear; an explicit re-dress action costs fixed time T_r. Solver outputs a permutation with optional re-dress insertions; verify.py simulates wear and scores total processing time. Trap: interleave-maintenance heuristics treat wear as monotone and pay T_r repeatedly, missing that sequencing soft jobs immediately before hard clusters is maintenance you were going to be paid to perform anyway.

innovation_hook: Redundancy must be placed by analyzing which cuts the scenario family collectively attacks (a small hitting set of threatened cuts), not by uniform edge thickening or per-scenario patching.
why_it_generalizes: Designing one artifact whose weak points are exactly what the evaluation ensemble probes teaches cut/coverage reasoning used in resilient infrastructure and fault-tolerant systems.
seed_example: gen.cpp outputs a weighted graph (n=3000) and K=12 failure scenarios, each a fixed edge-deletion set; the solver selects a subgraph of at most B edges; chk.cc scores sum over scenarios of (huge penalty per disconnected demand pair + total shortest-path stretch on surviving edges). Scenarios are generated to hit exactly the low-redundancy cuts of cheap spanners, so MST-plus-a-few-shortcuts gets disconnected repeatedly.

innovation_hook: Slots reward BATCHED crossings, so the winning move is to shape pushback times upstream to assemble full waves at the crossing — scheduling the queue, not the aircraft.
why_it_generalizes: Wave/batch formation against periodic shared windows is the core of memory-bus arbitration, traffic signal offsets, and synchronized IO flushing.
seed_example: Input (testlib gen): a taxiway graph with gates, one active runway that can only be crossed during periodic slots of width w every period p, and N aircraft with release times; an aircraft pushing back blocks its gate alley for d ticks. Output: a timed taxi route per aircraft. chk.cc replays, rejects overlapping occupancy, and scores Ratio from total weighted taxi delay. Trap: FCFS-priority routing sends aircraft to the crossing one at a time, wasting most of each slot and cascading gate-alley blocks.

innovation_hook: The optimal pin schedule is a shortest-path over (epoch, pin-set) states where you sometimes pin a key BEFORE it becomes hot to split one swap across two epochs — frequency ranking never reveals this.
why_it_generalizes: Amortizing reconfiguration cost against nonstationary demand is the core of index tuning, autoscaling, and JIT compilation decisions.

innovation_hook: Value lives in closed multi-hop material loops, so the search must be cycle-centric (negative-cost cycle canceling on a residual graph), not pairwise matching.
why_it_generalizes: Detecting and exploiting planted cyclic structure in exchange networks transfers to kidney-exchange-style problems, circular-economy design, and flow decomposition generally.
seed_example: gen.cpp emits N<=3000 plants, each consuming and producing typed material vectors with fixed conversion ratios; instances plant several long near-lossless cycles (A's waste is B's feed ... back to A) hidden among noise plants. Solver outputs directed exchange edges with flow rates under per-plant port limits; chk.cc balances all flows and scores waste avoided minus a transport tax, emitting Ratio against the planted-cycle bound. Trap: greedy bilateral best-pair matching captures 2-cycles but never assembles the long planted cycles that dominate the score.

innovation_hook: Global connectivity and perfect symmetry are topologically incompatible; the winning move is computing a minimum symmetry-breaking set of crossings that welds k congruent loops into one.
why_it_generalizes: Teaches diagnosing structural incompatibility between two desiderata and optimizing the exact locus of compromise instead of trading them off blindly.
seed_example: Input: n=15 dot grid, target rotation order k=4. Solver outputs an arc-tile choice at every lattice crossing such that the resulting curve system is ONE closed loop covering all edges; checker verifies single-loop via traversal, then scores (number of crossings whose tile agrees with its C4 orbit — fewer defects is better) plus entropy of the loop's turn-sequence 4-grams. Trap: making every orbit perfectly symmetric splits the curve into k congruent disjoint loops, failing the hard single-stroke constraint.

innovation_hook: Leaves sharing ancestors impose conflicting constraints, so the reachable output set is a cone — you must project onto it and prioritize the few inlets that feed many leaves.
why_it_generalizes: Constrained inversion of a shared-resource DAG recurs in supply, fluidics, and signal-mixing design.
seed_example: Input: a DAG whose sources are inlet tanks, internal nodes mix parents by fixed flow-fraction weights, and leaves are outputs; plus a target concentration vector (M species) at each leaf. Solver outputs a nonneg, capped concentration vector per inlet. Checker propagates concentrations through the DAG (each node = weighted average of parents) and scores sum of leaf ||achieved−target|| + cost·(total chemical injected). Trap: the leaf→inlet map is linear so an unconstrained least-squares solve returns negative or over-cap inlets that get clipped and wreck the match.

innovation_hook: Allowing intermediate cancellation, not just monotone subexpression reuse, unlocks shorter linear circuits than any greedy shared-sum heuristic finds.
why_it_generalizes: General lesson that the natural monotone search space is not the true optimum space; enlarging the move set changes the ceiling.
seed_example: Input = a target m x n GF(2) matrix M (m,n <= 40). The solver outputs a straight-line program of two-input XOR gates starting from the n inputs; the checker verifies the m outputs equal Mx for all x and scores the XOR count. Trap: emitting each output row as an independent XOR-fold uses ~density gates with no reuse; the win is discovering shared intermediate sums and allowing cancellation (x xor x = 0) to reach forms unreachable by cancellation-free sharing, driving the count below the row-by-row baseline.

innovation_hook: Balance the covariance of machine loads, not just their means — co-locating anti-correlated jobs cancels their peaks and lowers the worst-case load.
why_it_generalizes: Reasoning about second-order structure (variance/covariance) rather than point estimates transfers to any load-balancing, portfolio, or capacity-planning method.
seed_example: Each instance is a set of jobs whose per-run processing times are correlated via a hidden low-rank covariance; the program outputs an assignment of jobs to k machines. The evaluator computes the expected makespan (max machine load) over the hidden joint distribution. On trap instances jobs come in anti-correlated pairs, so balancing marginal means alone leaves one machine spiking whenever a pair co-activates.

innovation_hook: Pre-position firebreaks on the deterministic future frontier implied by wind and fuel, spending commitment before the fire arrives.
why_it_generalizes: Trains anticipation of a spreading process combined with irreversible resource commitment, transferring to containment, epidemic cordon, and any dispatch problem where deployment lead time matters.
seed_example: A seeded fire ignites and spreads deterministically to adjacent cells each step using per-cell fuel and wind rates read from the input; the program dispatches a fixed number of crews, each of which commits to building a firebreak in one cell for k steps and cannot redeploy during that window. The evaluator scores total unburned area at episode end across seeds. The trap: sending crews to the current flame edge is wasted because committed crews are locked while the front sweeps past them.

innovation_hook: The score depends on the change in transport capacity, not the flow itself: you are designing the derivative of the slope, engineering acceleration and then a stall where deposit is wanted.
why_it_generalizes: Rewards reformulating a placement task as controlling a rate-of-change field, a shift from optimizing states to optimizing gradients that transfers to many dynamical-design problems.
seed_example: gen.py emits a seeded 128x128 integer heightfield with a spring cell and a target deposition zone; the solver outputs up to V unit dig/fill edits. verify.py runs T steps of a fixed integer erosion CA (water routes to lowest neighbor, entrains/drops sediment by a capacity rule) and scores sediment mass deposited inside the target zone. Trap: carving a straight canal moves water fast — too fast to drop anything; you must design a slope profile that steepens first (to entrain) then flattens exactly at the zone (to stall and deposit).

innovation_hook: Estimate the dominant eigenvalue from successive residual ratios and set the relaxation factor per-instance, invoking Aitken extrapolation only when the mode is real.

Trap instances are near-critical with an oscillatory dominant mode where plain iteration crawls and a fixed over-relaxation diverges.

innovation_hook: Choose probes for their power to break the aliasing between the surviving hypotheses, not for marginal information under the current posterior.
why_it_generalizes: Trains value-of-information reasoning where each measurement reshapes which future measurements are informative, generalizing to experimental design, diagnostics, and adaptive testing.
seed_example: A seeded hidden parameter vector governs an unknown response surface aliased by a few competing hypotheses; each step the program picks one of many candidate measurements (each with a seeded cost and noise level) and receives the deterministic seeded-noisy result, updating its posterior, then after a fixed budget outputs a final estimate. The evaluator scores final estimate error plus total measurement cost across seeds. The trap: greedy maximum-information-per-cost keeps buying cheap redundant probes and never runs the one expensive probe that separates the two surviving aliased hypotheses.

innovation_hook: Lattice focusing is a number-theoretic problem of equalizing integer arrival times, so the best geometry deliberately deviates from the continuous ideal.
why_it_generalizes: Trains distrust of continuous-domain analogies when the actual evaluator is discrete — reading the simulator's true metric instead of importing textbook shapes.

innovation_hook: Optimal mass split is dictated by the downstream jettison-and-loss schedule, not by any single stage's local efficiency.

innovation_hook: When perfection is impossible the objective is defect routing, not local matching.

innovation_hook: Fault tolerance is a covering problem over deletions, not a duplication problem — shared 'mop-up' comparators amortize redundancy across many failure cases.
why_it_generalizes: Amortized redundancy (one guard covering many faults) is the key idea behind efficient error correction, RAID, and checkpointing schemes.
seed_example: Input: n (e.g., 16). Submission: a comparator sequence. counter.py verifies via the 0/1 principle that the network still sorts when any single comparator is deleted (the deletion sweep), then scores the comparator count; failing any deletion voids the submission. Trap: duplicating every comparator of an optimal network is valid at 2x size but far from the ceiling; strong solutions overlap fault coverage so one late comparator repairs many possible deletions at once.

innovation_hook: Damping effectiveness is non-monotone in local damper strength — there is a critical-damping sweet spot per mode — so the greedy 'hit the biggest amplitude' rule provably backfires and placement must trade off across the whole mode family.
why_it_generalizes: Non-monotone response to intervention strength (more medicine is not more cure) appears across control, regularization, and resource allocation; it forces modeling the mechanism instead of ranking by first-order sensitivity.
seed_example: Input: a mass-spring tree (n<=400 masses, given stiffnesses) and k identical viscous dampers. Output: the set of k nodes to damp. verify.py assembles the linearized 2n x 2n state matrix, computes all eigenvalues deterministically, and scores the decay margin -max Re(lambda) (higher is better). Trap: stacking dampers on the antinodes of the slowest mode — past a threshold the damped nodes dynamically detach (overdamping) and the mode's eigenvalue creeps back toward the imaginary axis; the optimum spreads moderate damping where several mode shapes overlap.

innovation_hook: A mixture of two short laws masquerades as one long law on any finite window — testing residue-class restrictions of the data reveals two clean small systems where one messy big one was assumed.
why_it_generalizes: Recognizing that apparent complexity is interleaved simplicity (and fitting robustly under corruption) transfers to log analysis, protocol reverse-engineering, and time-series modeling.
seed_example: gen.py prints an integer sequence a(1..N) generated by two different short linear recurrences, selected each step by n mod m for a hidden modulus m, with about 2% of entries replaced by corrupt values; the solver outputs the recurrences, m, and predictions for a(N+1..N+K). verify.py scores exact-match count on the K held-out terms (converted to an error) plus coefficient-count parsimony. Trap: Berlekamp-Massey or a single least-squares recurrence absorbs the switching into a long fake recurrence that fits N terms and derails within a few extrapolated steps, and the corrupted lines poison any non-robust fit.

innovation_hook: Empty slots are a first-class resource to be positioned, not leftover space: absent-lookup cost is governed by the empty-slot distribution, which couples all placements globally and kills per-key greedy reasoning.

Trap: Robin-Hood/greedy frequency-first placement fills contiguously and makes absent-heavy regions catastrophically expensive; deliberately leaving empty 'breakwater' slots inside hot hash regions is the winning move but sacrifices placements elsewhere.

innovation_hook: Profitable menus deliberately concede surplus to low risks -- the binding design question is which types you must let walk away, not how to price the ones you keep.
why_it_generalizes: Screening against a fully known but heterogeneous population trains reasoning about incentive-compatibility as geometry over the type cloud, applicable to tiered products, SLAs, and plan design.
seed_example: Input: 100k agents with risk p_i, risk-aversion a_i, and outside option u_i, all printed. Output: a menu of at most 6 (premium, deductible) contracts. verify.py has each agent pick its expected-utility-maximizing contract or exit, then scores premiums minus exact expected payouts. Trap: one contract priced at average risk collects only the high-risk tail and loses money -- the planted correlation between p_i and a_i makes the death spiral steep, and separating menus must respect a planted two-cluster type geometry rather than a smooth continuum.

innovation_hook: Temperature-2 cooperativity lets a handful of tiles encode a counter, so the tile count can be logarithmic if you build a machine instead of a map.
why_it_generalizes: Trains the substitution of computation for storage under a hard exact-output constraint, a canonical complexity insight.
seed_example: gen.py specifies a target finite shape (e.g. a filled square of side N or an N-wide staircase). Solver outputs a Wang tile set (glue labels + strengths) and a seed tile for the abstract Tile Assembly Model at temperature 2; verify.py simulates deterministic assembly, requires the unique terminal shape to equal the target, and scores the number of distinct tile types. Trap: one tile type per grid cell always works but is O(N^2), whereas the minimum uses cooperative binary-counter constructions that assemble side-N in O(log N) tile types.

innovation_hook: Register pressure, not dependence height, is the real bottleneck — treating recomputation as a scheduling primitive turns a spill cliff into free parallelism.
why_it_generalizes: Joint resource/ordering optimization with recompute-vs-hold tradeoffs is ubiquitous in compilers and workflow engines.
seed_example: gen.cpp emits a 10^5-node typed DAG plus a machine model: 4 issue slots per cycle with unit types and a 64-register file; solver prints a cycle-by-cycle schedule that may recompute nodes; chk.cc validates dependences, slot types, and register liveness, and emits Ratio from cycle count. Planted: many long independent chains braided together with rare coupling nodes. Trap: greedy list scheduling saturates registers mid-braid and serializes; unbraiding the chains plus rematerializing cheap nodes near their uses restores full slot occupancy.

innovation_hook: The mechanism is a hybrid automaton, not a function — the discovery step is inferring the mode-switching guard, after which each mode is trivially simple.
why_it_generalizes: Decomposing behavior into discrete modes with per-mode simple laws is how engineers model contacts, circuits, and software state machines.
seed_example: gen.py emits (applied_force[t], position[t]) traces of a block governed by static friction with a slow logarithmic creep term while stuck and kinetic friction while sliding, observed only under gentle quasi-steady pulls; the solver prints the two-branch law and the stick/slip switching condition. verify.py rolls the law on held-out aggressive pull-release profiles and scores position error plus term-count parsimony. Trap: a single viscous-friction linear model fits the gentle traces but predicts smooth motion where the true system stick-slips, getting even the qualitative event count wrong.

innovation_hook: Saturation makes stimulus value relative, so the winning sequence manufactures low states on purpose to buy slope where the peak-end score actually pays.
why_it_generalizes: Saturating-response scheduling with a peak-end objective appears in ad pacing, curriculum pacing, and drug dosing; anti-greedy valley construction is the transferable insight.
seed_example: gen.py emits songs with energy e_i and style vectors; crowd state updates E += e_i*(1-E) - fatigue, where fatigue grows when consecutive styles are similar; score = alpha*max(E over time) + (1-alpha)*E_final. Output: an ordered subset of songs fitting a time budget; verify.py replays the dynamics deterministically. Trap: greedy highest-energy-first saturates E early and forfeits the peak-end score; strong solutions engineer deliberate valleys that reset crowd sensitivity right before a planted climax window.

innovation_hook: Regret scoring changes what matters: scenarios that are easy in absolute cost can dominate the ratio, so you must identify which scenario is regret-critical and buy its spike corridor even though it carries little average demand.
why_it_generalizes: Minimax-regret against per-scenario baselines is the standard honest metric for robust design and punishes exactly the pooled-average shortcut models reach for first.
seed_example: gen.cpp emits a street graph, a length budget, K=6 origin-destination demand matrices (weekday/weekend/event/...), and a precomputed per-scenario oracle cost (from a strong scenario-specific construction, shipped in the input); the solver outputs L line paths; chk.cc computes demand-weighted travel time per scenario and scores the maximum RATIO to that scenario's oracle. Trap: fitting lines to the summed OD matrix is nearly optimal for weekday regret but 3x regret on the event scenario's spike corridor.

innovation_hook: The central mutex makes ALTERNATION the real resource: near-ceiling plans pre-compute an interleaved cadence where one arm sweeps the center only during the other arm's flank strokes, i.e., you schedule the mutex, not the items.
why_it_generalizes: Scheduling access to a contended region as a first-class periodic object generalizes to bus arbitration, critical sections, and shared-airspace corridor slotting.
seed_example: Input: items appear on a belt at known positions/times and exit the reachable window after a known interval; two arms have overlapping reachable regions, and both being inside the shared central region simultaneously is forbidden; each pick occupies the arm for a move-time given by an exact distance table. Output: per arm, a timed list of picks. The checker replays, rejects mutex or reach violations, and scores total value of items picked before exit. Trap: both arms greedily chasing the soonest-expiring items collide in the (deliberately item-rich) central region and spend most ticks yielding.

innovation_hook: Skip placement is a coupled facility-location problem across queries sharing lists — the useful positions are derivable only by simulating the fixed traversal, turning the checker's own algorithm into the design tool.
why_it_generalizes: Auxiliary-structure budgets (indexes, samples, shortcuts) should be spent where the executing algorithm actually stalls, a transferable profile-then-place methodology.
seed_example: Input (testlib): 200 sorted posting lists (total 2e6 ints) and 5e3 fixed AND queries over pairs/triples of lists, plus a global budget of B skip pointers. Solver prints, per list, the positions holding skips (and their targets); checker runs one FIXED deterministic intersection algorithm (advance via skip when it does not overshoot, else linear) and counts every element and skip probe. Trap: textbook sqrt(n)-spaced skips ignore that probes concentrate exactly where the partner list's values land; computing those landing points from the actual query set and spending the budget there beats uniform spacing by a wide margin, but budget spent on one list starves another query.

innovation_hook: A timeline is a product of maps — the score rewards recognizing algebraic structure (periodicity plus bandedness) instead of simulating time, with a strict exact-arithmetic correctness gate.
why_it_generalizes: Replacing simulation with algebraic composition and structure-aware powering is a core computational-science insight (Markov chains, linear recurrences, circuit folding).
seed_example: gen.py emits a chain of S storage nodes evolving by per-step affine conversion updates whose coefficient blocks repeat with a planted period p plus a few irregular steps, over horizon T~1e5. Submission is a straight-line add/mul program computing the exact final state; counter.py replays it in rational arithmetic, requires a bit-exact match with reference simulation, and scores the operation count. Trap: step-by-step simulation costs O(T*S); composing affine maps and binary-powering the periodic block reaches O(S^3 log T), and exploiting the planted banded coupling of the chain beats that again.

innovation_hook: Only a match of the full reflectance spectrum shape — not a single tristimulus point — buys illuminant-invariance.
why_it_generalizes: Matching a latent function rather than a projected observation defeats aliasing in any multi-view inverse problem.

Trap: achieving a metameric match under one illuminant leaves large ΔE under the others.

innovation_hook: The dense feasible configurations are algebraic (difference sets from cyclic groups), invisible to any incremental collision-avoidance heuristic.
why_it_generalizes: Recognizing when the optimum lives in an algebraic family rather than a search neighborhood is a deep modeling move.

Trap: greedy 'add the heaviest cell that keeps differences distinct' plateaus far below the algebraic ceiling reachable by a Singer/perfect-difference-set construction folded onto the torus dimensions.

innovation_hook: Spend a small pilot batch to estimate each region's standard deviation, then allocate the rest proportional to region-sd times region-size (Neyman allocation) rather than uniformly.
why_it_generalizes: Pilot-then-allocate proportional to uncertainty is the reusable core of adaptive sampling, active measurement, and stratified estimation.

The evaluator computes squared error against the true integral. Trap instances concentrate almost all variance in a few small regions, so uniform allocation wastes the budget on flat regions.

innovation_hook: Trade only spreads wide enough to clear the marginal aging cost and shallow-cycle to preserve capacity for the few large seeded price swings.
why_it_generalizes: Teaches trading a consumable asset whose use degrades its future capability, transferring to storage, equipment utilization, and any control where exercising capacity today reduces it tomorrow.
seed_example: A seeded price series and a battery with round-trip efficiency loss; each step the program charges or discharges within power limits, and cumulative cycling deterministically degrades capacity as a function of depth-of-discharge, raising future losses. The evaluator scores arbitrage profit minus a degradation cost tied to how deep each cycle went, across seeds. The trap: buying low and selling high on every local price spread over-cycles the battery so aging cost plus efficiency loss erases the gains.

innovation_hook: Delay makes optimal control anticipatory: you must spill before inflow peaks, accepting local loss for global peak-shaving across coupled reservoirs.
why_it_generalizes: Trains writing feedback policies that act against instantaneous gradients because of system lag — a core control insight for queues, caches, and pipelines.
seed_example: evaluator.py holds 50 seeded inflow hydrographs and a deterministic 3-reservoir cascade model with travel-time delays; the submitted program reads current storages and recent inflows each tick and prints gate settings. Score = mean downstream flood peak over the 50 episodes (minimize). Trap: the greedy 'release only when nearly full' policy synchronizes all three dams' emergency spills into one superflood; good policies pre-release from the downstream dam to create absorption headroom before the routed pulse arrives, which looks locally wasteful.

innovation_hook: Ignition points are chosen for the closure of the propagation graph they unlock, so a sparse well-placed set beats a dense obvious one.
why_it_generalizes: Seeding a deterministic cascade to reach protected targets models influence maximization, fault propagation, and activation routing.
seed_example: gen.cpp emits a grid of destructible blocks with values and trigger radii, plus shielded target blocks reachable only through chains. The solver picks M ignition cells (committed). chk.cc detonates: each explosion triggers all blocks within its radius, propagating simultaneously to fixed point, and reports destroyed target value. Trap: igniting the densest clusters maximizes immediate blast but not reach; the optimum places few ignitions at cells whose deterministic trigger-closure routes through the shield gaps to the targets — you must reason about the reachability closure of the trigger graph, not local density.

innovation_hook: Heat budgets convert edge-sharing from a max-weight matching into a bin-constrained clustering where splitting savings across several small contacts dominates taking the single best pair.
why_it_generalizes: Sharing an expense between neighbors under per-node exposure caps is the structure of cost-sharing networks, cache line sharing, and co-scheduling.
seed_example: Input: rectilinear parts with per-part heat budgets h_i and edge lists; placing two parts flush lets one cut serve both, saving its length, but a shared cut charges its full length to both parts' heat budgets while a solo cut charges half. Solver outputs a placement on the sheet; the checker detects coincident edges, verifies non-overlap and budgets, and scores total cut length saved. Trap: greedily pairing the longest edges exhausts the budgets of exactly the parts with the most sharable perimeter; the strong solution treats it as a degree-constrained b-matching where fragile parts take many short shared edges instead of one long one.
