innovation_hook: Growth is killed exactly by proper-subgroup containment, and SL2's subgroups are classified — so certifying that the pair generates (checking orders and trace conditions against the Dickson classification) is the real optimization, after which near-free growth 2k(2k-1)^{r-1} follows.
why_it_generalizes: Optimizing an expansion objective by excluding classified algebraic failure modes instead of blind search mirrors real expander and mixing constructions.
seed_example: Input: a prime p and k (e.g. k=2,3); solver prints k matrices in SL2(F_p); verify.py runs deterministic BFS from the identity and scores the number of distinct group elements within radius r (r fixed so full group is unreachable). Trap: commuting or low-order generators, or generators trapped in a Borel/dihedral subgroup, give polynomial ball growth; naive random-looking picks often land in structured subgroups for small p.

innovation_hook: State-adjacency-aware encoding collapses transition logic that lexicographic numbering leaves dense.
why_it_generalizes: Cost-aware representation choice (not just correctness) transfers to compilation and layout problems.
seed_example: Input: a seeded Mealy/DFA transition function (states, input symbols, next state). innovation_hook: The coarse partition must be chosen for the fine partitions it will permit, since committed bins can only be split, never moved.

Trap: choosing the coarse layer to minimize coarse-layer error locks in bin boundaries whose subdivisions can never straddle the true values; the optimum places coarse boundaries to anticipate where fine subdivision will need to land.

innovation_hook: The chatter constraint makes removal order change future per-pass capacity, so the optimal tape interleaves bands to preserve stiffness where deep cuts are still pending.
why_it_generalizes: Straight-line plans whose step capacities depend on what earlier steps consumed model battery-aware routing, decommissioning sequences, and register-pressure-aware code scheduling.
seed_example: Input: a target staircase profile (radii per axial band) turned from a cylinder held at one end, a tool catalog (each tool removes at most depth d_t per pass and cannot reach into corners tighter than its nose radius), and a chatter rule: a pass at axial distance x with depth d is legal only if x*d <= K given currently remaining material. Submission is a straight-line program of passes and tool changes; counter.py verifies precedence (outer material must go first), the chatter rule at execution time, final profile exactness, and counts passes + C per tool change as the exact score. Trap: minimizing passes per band independently ignores that removing distal material early destroys the stiffness (remaining cross-section) that later deep proximal passes need, so the cheap-looking order forces many shallow passes.

innovation_hook: A perfect tile inside a union of H-cosets must be (up to defect) a cross-section construction: pick one element per coset pattern so that B's mask polynomial B(x) satisfies B(x)*T(x)=1+x+...+x^{n-1} mod x^n-1 — checking candidate tiles by their zeros on n-th roots of unity prunes the search to a tiny algebraic family.
why_it_generalizes: Links a packing objective to polynomial zero conditions on roots of unity, a transferable certificate technique for exact-cover-style problems.
seed_example: gen.py gives composite n, tile size k with k|n, and an allowed set A subset of Z_n (planted as a noisy union of subgroup cosets); solver prints a tile B subset of A with |B|=k plus n/k translation offsets; verify.py counts residues covered != exactly once (minimize, 0 = perfect tiling). Trap: interval tiles are forbidden by A, and greedy tile-growing gets stuck at a large defect because tiling-ness is a global algebraic property.

innovation_hook: Fidelity is not a transport/propagation property but a spectral-arithmetic one — perfect transfer happens iff eigenvalue gaps are commensurate — so the solver must design the spectrum, not the path.
why_it_generalizes: Recasting a dynamical objective as a static constraint on eigenvalue arithmetic is a deep, transferable move (revivals, echoes, aliasing all share it).
seed_example: Input: a path of N sites with a few sites carrying fixed defect masses and a few couplings frozen; time horizon T. Output: the remaining coupling strengths (bounded reals, fixed decimal precision). verify.py computes the matrix exponential exp(iAT) deterministically and scores transfer fidelity |exp(iAT)_{1,N}|. Trap: uniform or locally-smoothed couplings let dispersion smear the packet to near-zero fidelity; high fidelity requires engineering an (approximately) mirror-symmetric matrix with near-linear eigenvalue spacing (couplings ~ sqrt(k(N-k))), adapted around the frozen defects.

innovation_hook: Regeneration is impossible without an internally reconstructable coordinate system, so the rule must maintain positional information, not just a shape.

innovation_hook: The H = 2m*R decomposition converts 'add shortcuts' into a ratio game: an edge helps only if its resistance reduction beats its mass dilution, which reverses the intuitive ranking of candidate shortcuts.

innovation_hook: The unknown is an operator, not a set of curves — sparsity plus shared-operator structure means each observed trajectory constrains every future experiment.
why_it_generalizes: Recovering a shared latent coupling structure from few interventions is the template for causal discovery, network tomography, and epidemic tracing.
seed_example: gen.py provides node-temperature time series of linear diffusion x[t+1]=A x[t] on a hidden sparse graph (n=30, ~2.5 avg degree), observed for impulses injected at only 5 source nodes; the solver outputs the weighted edge list. verify.py injects impulses at held-out source nodes, rolls the submitted graph, and scores trajectory error plus an edge-count parsimony term. Trap: fitting independent per-node decay curves or a dense unregularized A reproduces the 5 training impulses but transfers wrong to new sources; recognizing that all responses share ONE sparse operator makes 5 impulses vastly overdetermine it.

innovation_hook: Broken ice is a decaying shared asset, so the plan should maximize reuse of recent work, making temporal corridor alignment worth large spatial detours.
why_it_generalizes: Decaying-benefit-of-work over a shared network models trail maintenance, cache warming, and knowledge freshness; converting it to a space-time reuse problem is a strong abstraction.
seed_example: gen.cpp emits a channel graph with per-edge ice regrowth toward a cap, plus convoys (origin, destination, ready time); breaking edge e at thickness x costs x^1.5 and thickness restarts growing after passage. Output: timed routes for the single icebreaker and every convoy; chk.cc simulates thickness over time and sums energy. Trap: escorting each convoy along its static shortest path treats edges as fixed-cost; the winning structure is wave scheduling, batching convoys through freshly broken corridors inside the regrowth window, effectively a TTL cache laid over space-time.

innovation_hook: The rendezvous requirement makes team formation the real variable — solve a crew-pairing problem (persistent tug teams routed through job chains) instead of per-ship assignment.
why_it_generalizes: Recognizing that simultaneity constraints demand persistent coalitions, not per-task matching, transfers to multi-robot lifting, surgical team scheduling, and gang scheduling on clusters.

innovation_hook: The two stores serve different probability strata — the lossy store's optimal steady state is 'nearly always idle', which every efficiency metric calls waste; recognizing insurance as its job is the leap.

innovation_hook: Window coverage is an Eulerian-path problem on the de Bruijn graph; forbidden windows are deleted edges you must balance before a tour exists.
why_it_generalizes: Recasting a sequence-construction task as a graph-tour problem is a reusable structural insight.
seed_example: Input = alphabet size, window length L, and a set of forbidden length-L windows. The solver outputs a (possibly cyclic) string that contains every allowed L-window at least once and no forbidden window; verify.py checks coverage and legality and scores string length (shorter better). Trap: greedily extending by 'append a symbol yielding a new window' dead-ends into long strings; modeling it as an Eulerian path on the (L-1)-mer de Bruijn subgraph with forbidden edges removed gives near-optimal length, but only after repairing the subgraph to be Eulerian.

innovation_hook: Treat mid-acuity patients about to tip into critical, using the deterministic escalation curve to prevent crises rather than react to them.

innovation_hook: At steady state damping effectiveness is proportional to modal amplitude at the panel, making far-from-listener placements the strongest moves.
why_it_generalizes: Teaches that in resonant/feedback systems interventions matter where the system's eigenstructure is large, not where the symptom is observed — a diagnosis pattern with wide reach.

Trap: piling absorbers around the listener barely helps because the room's standing modes pump energy everywhere; treating mode antinodes and the few first-reflection wall cells (computable by mirror-image geometry) dominates.

Trap: the load-optimal final truss is often un-buildable bottom-up because an intermediate cantilever collapses; you must co-design the member set and the addition order so every prefix satisfies static equilibrium.

innovation_hook: The pin budget prices the boundary of the decomposition while sheet waste prices its interior, an isoperimetric trade a per-feature greedy never sees.
why_it_generalizes: Partitioning a domain so each block uses its locally best representation, paying for inter-block boundaries, is exactly domain decomposition, hybrid mesh generation, and columnar-vs-row storage layout.
seed_example: Input: a voxel solid and stock sheets of size W x H (slab thickness = 1 voxel). Solver partitions the solid into axis-aligned regions, assigns each region a slicing axis, emits every slab outline, and nests all slabs onto sheets; verify.py re-stacks the slabs to confirm the exact solid is rebuilt, charges one alignment pin per unit area of interface between differently-axed regions against a pin budget P, and scores sheets consumed. Trap: one global slicing direction (the obvious baseline) explodes sheet count on thin features orthogonal to it, while slicing every feature optimally shatters the solid into interfaces that blow the pin budget — the score rewards finding the few large regions where switching axis pays for its interface.

innovation_hook: Border sets obey correlation-polynomial constraints, so naive prefix-suffix stitching leaks spurious periods.
why_it_generalizes: Respecting hidden feasibility conditions of a combinatorial object generalizes to constrained construction broadly.
seed_example: Input: a target set B of desired border lengths (proper prefixes equal to suffixes) and an alphabet, seeded from a realizable correlation. Trap: naively stitching prefix=suffix segments creates unintended extra borders, because a correlation set is not freely composable. The insight is to use the correlation-polynomial forward/backward consistency conditions to place letters so exactly the target borders exist and none else.

innovation_hook: The sweep is white-box, so the task inverts hashing: characterize each family's invariant, then compose mixers so no family's invariant survives all stages — design by adversary decomposition, not by average avalanche.
why_it_generalizes: Decomposing an adversary suite into invariants and building layered defenses with disjoint blind spots generalizes to defense-in-depth across systems and crypto engineering.

Trap: any single multiplicative or xor-shift mixer has resonant strides present in the sweep; the ceiling composes stages whose individual weaknesses are provably disjoint across the published family list, and spends the salt table exactly on the residual worst families.

innovation_hook: Head loss scaling as flow-squared over diameter-to-the-fifth makes trunk-line diameter strongly convex in value, inverting the intuition of even per-pipe sizing.
why_it_generalizes: Quadratic flow-pressure coupling governs water, gas, and HVAC network design broadly.
seed_example: Input: a pipe network with a source, node demands, and discrete diameter options per pipe under a cost budget. Solver picks diameters; verify.py solves the nonlinear hydraulic equations (head loss proportional to flow^2/diameter^5, Weymouth-style) for a consistent flow/pressure field and reports total unmet demand (minimize). Trap: sizing each pipe to its linear demand share (as in a tree) starves the system because head loss is quadratic in flow and drops steeply with diameter, so budget must concentrate on fat trunk lines and loops that relieve pressure nonlinearly.

innovation_hook: Only the true rational function, not a per-length fit, survives the longer held-out regime.
why_it_generalizes: Extracting a generative mechanism instead of a lookup is the essence of scientific generalization.
seed_example: Input (E): input-output string pairs of a hidden finite-state transducer, split into a TRAIN regime of short inputs and a held-out EXTRAPOLATION regime of longer inputs. The solver outputs a transducer; the checker verifies train consistency then scores exact-match accuracy on the longer held-out inputs. Trap: memorizing the train table or fitting per-length rules matches train but collapses on longer inputs. The insight is to recover the underlying subsequential rational function (onward PTT minimization) whose mechanism extends to any length.

innovation_hook: A worst-of scoring rule converts frequency mining into an equalization problem — dictionary space must be allocated by each domain's marginal worst-case gain, and cross-domain substrings are worth double their frequency suggests.
why_it_generalizes: Budgeted resource equalization across heterogeneous consumers under a min objective is ubiquitous, from multi-tenant capacity to multi-task model capacity.

Trap: filling the dictionary with globally most-frequent substrings overfits the largest domain and the worst-document score collapses; the ceiling finds cross-domain shared stems and gives each domain a marginal-gain-balanced quota, equalizing the minimum.

innovation_hook: SNR is unimodal in launch power (linear ASE gain versus cubic Kerr penalty), so there is an interior optimal power rather than 'more is better'.
why_it_generalizes: The signal-versus-nonlinear-noise unimodality recurs in amplification, power control, and any gain-with-nonlinear-cost system.
seed_example: Input: an optical link topology with span lengths, discrete amplifier placements, and launch-power levels. Solver chooses amplifier spacing and per-span power; verify.py accumulates linear amplifier (ASE) noise plus Kerr nonlinear noise proportional to power^3 per span, computes each node-pair SNR, and reports the count of pairs above the SNR threshold (maximize). Trap: cranking power to the max or spacing amplifiers evenly fails because the cubic nonlinear penalty overwhelms the linear signal gain past an optimal launch power.

innovation_hook: You cannot outrun a comb by shifting the spectrum — you must tear holes in it; recognizing that periodic modulation creates band gaps converts a hopeless chase into a direct construction.
why_it_generalizes: The shift-versus-gap distinction (move the system vs restructure its spectrum) applies to interference avoidance in scheduling, communications, and materials.
seed_example: Input: a discretized beam of S segments, a stiffness budget to allocate across segments (integer units), and a comb of forcing frequencies with weights. Output: per-segment stiffness. verify.py computes all eigenfrequencies deterministically and scores the total resonant amplification sum_f w_f / (min_j |omega_f - omega_j| + eps). Trap: uniform stiffening rescales the whole spectrum, keeping the same collisions with the evenly spaced forcing comb (the instances plant the comb at the uniform-beam spacing); the escape is periodic stiffness modulation that opens band gaps exactly at comb frequencies rather than shifting modes wholesale.

innovation_hook: A cost model that exponentially punishes depth makes the canonical optimal algorithm the worst viable answer, forcing genuine restructuring rather than recall.
why_it_generalizes: Re-deriving what is optimal under a shifted cost model (instead of pattern-matching to cached optima) is exactly the transfer skill RL should amplify.

Trap: Horner's rule — the universally correct answer for minimizing multiplications — has linear depth and thus an exponentially bad score; the solver must recognize that the objective inverts the usual optimality.

innovation_hook: The machines interfere through a shared environmental state, so optimal parallelism is an interior optimum computable from the congestion law, not maximal fan-out.
why_it_generalizes: Shared-resource congestion feedback (thermal budgets, bandwidth, shared coolant) is everywhere; learning that parallelism has an interior optimum breaks a very common greedy recipe.
seed_example: gen.py emits P panels, each an ordered chain of coats with base drying times; the actual drying rate of every wet coat is divided by (1 + c*number_currently_wet), and a single worker applies coats instantaneously at chosen times. Trap: starting every panel immediately is the obvious throughput move, but the congestion term makes drying effectively quadratic in parallelism; staggered cohorts whose size depends on c and chain position dominate, and the best stagger varies along the chains.

innovation_hook: Set tolls to steer the equilibrium toward the system-optimal split by anticipating the crowd's best-response, rather than damping the current hotspot.

innovation_hook: The count growth is governed by an incidence-matrix eigenvalue, so a polynomial fit is doomed to extrapolate wrong.
why_it_generalizes: Recovering the spectral mechanism behind data is a transferable law-discovery skill.
seed_example: Input (E): a short prefix of an infinite word generated by an unknown morphism plus observables (factor-count growth, letter-frequency vector) on that prefix; held-out predictions concern letter counts and specified factors far along a much longer prefix. Trap: fitting a polynomial or short linear recurrence to the observed counts matches the train prefix but diverges, because true growth is the Perron eigenvalue of the incidence matrix. The insight is to infer the substitution whose incidence-matrix spectrum reproduces the observed exponential growth.

why_it_generalizes: Designing partitions whose pairwise unions stay bounded is a strictly harder and more transferable skill than single-failure isolation, appearing in fault domains and shard placement.

Trap: putting valves around high-demand mains ignores that the lost quantity is the whole segment's demand; the ceiling is a demand-balanced segmentation of the graph, and the published double-burst list deliberately merges naive adjacent segments, breaking per-pipe reasoning.

innovation_hook: The rounding residue sequence b*(1+r) mod 1 is a rotation on the circle whose pattern reveals r exactly — the 'noise' is the signal.
why_it_generalizes: Exact reverse-engineering of quantized update rules transfers to fixed-point firmware, financial-system audits, and floating-point bug forensics.
seed_example: gen.py shows exact integer (in coins) daily balances evolving by b<-floor(b*(1+r)) plus a fee subtracted only in months where the minimum balance dipped below a hidden threshold, for several accounts over 200 days; the solver outputs the exact update rule (r as a fraction, fee, threshold). verify.py rolls the rule 1000 further days from held-out starting balances and scores the count of exactly-matching entries (as an error) plus parameter parsimony. Trap: a continuous compound-interest fit is within a coin per day yet drifts by hundreds of coins over 1000 days because floor() interacts with r's denominator to create periodic orbits; getting r's exact rational form from the rounding residues is the real problem.

innovation_hook: The system is a chain of coupled oscillators, and the score is governed by the spectral stability of your control law — policies that damp the slowest antisymmetric mode (hold proportional to BOTH neighbor gaps) beat any target-schedule chaser.
why_it_generalizes: Diagnosing an instability mode and designing feedback against it, rather than optimizing the visible objective myopically, is core scientist behavior found in queueing, thermal control, and training-dynamics stabilization.
seed_example: Submission: a Python holding policy (called whenever a bus reaches a stop, returns extra hold ticks) run by a frozen deterministic simulator: passengers accumulate at seeded rates, dwell time grows linearly with boarders, so a late bus gets later — an unstable feedback the generator seeds near criticality. Score: mean squared passenger wait over the horizon, exact replay. Trap: the myopic policy 'never hold, minimize this bus's dwell' maximizes instability and ends with all buses in one clump; naive constant-headway holding overcorrects and bleeds capacity.

innovation_hook: Latency is endogenous: the schedule chooses its own memory latencies through set conflicts, so the classic fixed-latency list-scheduling abstraction is the trap itself.

The trap: always grazing the greenest paddock overgrazes clusters and thrashes the herd across the grid paying repeated move costs.

innovation_hook: The true metric is soil-dependent required-depth-squared, so route and depth must be optimized jointly in a warped cost space where straight lines lie.
why_it_generalizes: Trains constructing the correct induced metric from environment dynamics before running any shortest-path reflex — metric-first reasoning that transfers widely.
seed_example: evaluator.py holds 40 seeded winter temperature series and a deterministic soil-column model where frost depth evolves per series and per surface cover; the submitted program reads a map (buildings to connect, soil and cover types) and outputs a pipe route with per-segment burial depth. Score = total excavation cost (depth squared per cell), with a huge penalty if any pipe cell freezes in any winter. Trap: burying everything at the depth of the worst frost is safe but pays quadratic cost everywhere; frost penetrates far more slowly under snow-holding and peat cells, so a longer shallow route through protective ground beats the geometric shortest path.

innovation_hook: The right wall is the future min-cut ridge that isolates the protected basin at high water, placed before the level rises to it.
why_it_generalizes: Staged irreversible barrier placement against a rising deterministic threshold models flood control, containment, and progressive isolation.

Between stages the solver places committed impermeable walls; then water floods deterministically to all cells below the current level connected to a source. chk.cc reports protected-zone cells kept dry minus forbidden cells flooded. Trap: walling the currently wettest boundary each stage wastes budget on already-doomed low ground; the optimum spends early budget on the elevation choke (min-cut ridge) that will separate the protected basin once the level rises, anticipating the future flood front.

innovation_hook: The train/test split is engineered so the dominant mechanism flips across it, making regime detection — not fit quality — the scored skill.
why_it_generalizes: Detecting that a system has phases and locating the boundary from one-sided data is a canonical scientific-inference move, from materials to epidemiology.
seed_example: gen.py emits bending experiments (die angle, sheet thickness t, die radius r) -> measured springback, generated from a hidden two-regime law: elastic-dominated springback ~ c1*r/t below a critical r/t, plastic-saturated ~ c2*(r/t)^{1/3} + c3 above it, with the crossover itself depending on t; training data spans thin sheets only, the held-out split is thick sheets where nearly all points sit in the other regime. Submission is a predictor program; verify.py scores held-out error only. Trap: a single smooth fit (or symbolic regression without a branch) interpolates the training cloud beautifully and extrapolates into the wrong regime; the necessary discovery is that there are two mechanisms and a t-dependent boundary between them.

innovation_hook: Split the unknowns by their timescales and spend measurement budget inversely to stability — a form/coefficient factorization turns a hard tracking problem into two easy ones.
why_it_generalizes: Separating stable structure from drifting parameters is the practical core of model maintenance, sensor calibration, and non-stationary forecasting.
seed_example: evaluator.py simulates a hidden law y=f(x; theta(t)) whose functional FORM is fixed but whose two parameters drift deterministically (one linear, one slow sinusoid) over a discrete time axis of 1000 ticks; the submitted program is charged one tick per probe (x of its choice, noisy y back) and must output predictions for a frozen set of (x, t_future) queries at ticks 1000-1500, scored by accuracy averaged over seeded instances. Trap: probing densely early and fitting a static model captures f but not the drift; spreading probes uniformly captures drift but starves the form-fitting — the insight is to identify the time-invariant FORM cheaply, then dedicate the remaining schedule to the two drifting coefficients, which need only sparse, well-spaced samples to extrapolate.

innovation_hook: With intransitive outcomes, seeding must carve a beatable path through the whole bracket rather than merely spreading strong players apart.

innovation_hook: The object being optimized is the worst case of your own future certificate, turning measurement placement into a minimax game against the bound formula rather than a coverage problem.
why_it_generalizes: Budgeted adaptive sampling to tighten a sound worst-case certificate is the skeleton of verification-guided testing, safe Bayesian optimization, and sensor placement.
seed_example: evaluator.py holds seeded manufactured surfaces z(x,y) (smooth base + localized defects, known Lipschitz constant Lc published in the spec); the submitted program adaptively chooses up to n probe points (each returns exact z), then must output a certified bound D such that true max|z - nominal| <= D; score per instance = max(0, 1 - D/D_ref) if the bound is valid, 0 if any instance's bound is violated, averaged over the fixed instance set. Trap: uniform grid probing wastes budget on flat regions and forces a loose Lipschitz-padded bound; the insight is that the certificate itself (Lc times distance-to-nearest-probe) tells you where the bound is weakest, so probing should greedily attack the argmax of the current certified envelope — and defect priors from early probes should re-weight it.

innovation_hook: Placing energy at target harmonics requires inverting the Bessel sideband structure that governs FM spectra.
why_it_generalizes: Inverting a known special-function forward map (Bessel here) to shape an output spectrum is a reusable analytic-inversion skill.
seed_example: Input: a fixed FM synth topology (e.g. 3 operators with given modulation routing) and a target magnitude spectrum (harmonic amplitudes up to H). Solver outputs each operator's frequency ratio and modulation index. Checker synthesizes the steady-state spectrum via the Bessel-function expansion of the FM signal and scores spectral error over harmonics + cost·(total modulation energy). Trap: matching the carrier ratio to the fundamental and ignoring modulation misses that sideband amplitudes follow Bessel functions of the index, not a simple sum.

innovation_hook: Under multiplicative per-cell loss the right surrogate is log-loss path length, and equalizing it means the farthest users get the straightest paths — inverting shortest-network intuition.
why_it_generalizes: Teaches that changing the aggregation (sum to min) changes the optimal topology class entirely, training sensitivity to score shape rather than to the domain's habitual solution.
seed_example: gen.py emits a field grid with soil infiltration rates, one spring of fixed discharge, F named fields, and a ditch-length budget; the solver outputs a ditch network with gate split ratios. verify.py runs the deterministic flow-and-seepage sim (water leaks per cell traversed) and scores the MINIMUM moisture across fields (maximize). Trap: the Steiner-tree shortest network maximizes total delivered water yet starves the far field, because multiplicative losses compound along its long unique path; maximin rewards loops and deliberately unequal splits favoring distant users.

innovation_hook: Fold order is gated by which creases stay accessible and by the fold-parity invariant, so late folds must be planned before early ones bury them.
why_it_generalizes: Order-dependent layering with a combinatorial feasibility invariant models sheet metal, packaging, and any stack-and-block process.
seed_example: Input: a 1D strip of unit segments with marked crease positions and a target flat-folded silhouette (segment stacking with a layer order). The solver outputs an ordered sequence of mountain/valley folds. Each fold is committed and stacks layers, blocking creases now buried under other layers from being folded later. The checker simulates the flat-folding and scores silhouette overlap plus correct layer ordering. Trap: folding to match the outline greedily buries a crease that a later required fold needs; feasibility obeys a mountain/valley parity (Maekawa/Kawasaki) invariant, so the crease order must respect both layer-access and parity.

innovation_hook: The cubic nonlinearity between removed material and compliance means curvature shaping is an inverse-design problem where the greedy monotone intuition (more cuts = more bend there) hits the strength wall first.
why_it_generalizes: Inverse design through a nonlinear local response with a survivability floor is the pattern of metamaterial design, antenna shaping, and dose planning.
seed_example: Input: a strip of m x k cells, a target curvature profile kappa(x) along the strip, and a deterministic model: local bending compliance of column x = 1/(sum of remaining ligament widths)^3, curvature realized = M * compliance for fixed moment M; every column's net remaining section must stay >= S. Solver outputs which cells to slit; verify.py computes the realized curvature profile and scores sum |kappa_realized - kappa_target| with hard rejection below strength S. Trap: uniform slit density (the standard living-hinge pattern) only produces constant curvature; matching a varying profile requires solving the inverse cubic-compliance problem, and the strength floor forbids the naive fix of simply slitting more where more bend is needed.

innovation_hook: By Lam-Leung, vanishing sums of n-th roots decompose into rotated full prime cycles, so the slack in any representation is a nonnegative combination of p-term relations — the search reduces to integer programming over the prime-relation lattice, not over angles.
why_it_generalizes: Exact sparse representation in a structured ring, with the relation lattice as the pruning tool, is the algebraic core of many minimal-description problems.
seed_example: gen.py fixes composite n (e.g. n=2*3*5*7) and a target cyclotomic integer given exactly by its coordinates in the power basis of Z[zeta_n]; solver prints a multiset of exponents e_1..e_t and verify.py checks sum(zeta_n^{e_i}) equals the target exactly in Z[x]/Phi_n(x); score = t (minimize). Trap: greedy angle-matching in floating point either fails the exact check or pads with many canceling pairs, landing far above the sparse optimum.

innovation_hook: Synchronization onset is a nonlinear threshold governed by the coupling Laplacian's spectral gap against local chaotic divergence, so budget must widen the gap, not feed hubs.
why_it_generalizes: Threshold synchronization underlies power-grid stability, neural and sensor coordination, and consensus dynamics.
seed_example: Input: a network of chaotic logistic maps and a total edge-coupling budget. Solver assigns coupling strengths to edges; verify.py iterates the coupled maps deterministically for T steps from a fixed seed and reports the number of nodes whose trajectory locks to the network mean within epsilon (maximize). Trap: spreading coupling uniformly or loading it onto hubs leaves the network below the synchronization threshold; onset is a nonlinear threshold set jointly by the coupling-Laplacian spectral gap and the maps' local instability.

innovation_hook: The right objective lives in the Laplacian eigenbasis, not on edges: an assignment is good iff its frequency vector avoids the slow eigenvector subspace, which often means deliberately mixing dissimilar frequencies locally.
why_it_generalizes: Assignment problems where quality is a global operator norm rather than a sum of local affinities break the matching/clustering reflex and reward basis-change reasoning.
seed_example: Input: a graph and a fixed multiset of natural frequencies (one per node, sum zero). Output: an assignment of frequencies to nodes. verify.py computes the linearized synchrony stress ||B^T L^+ omega||_inf (B incidence, L^+ pseudoinverse) exactly and scores it (lower = easier synchronization). Trap: greedily seating similar frequencies adjacently minimizes local mismatch but loads the bottleneck edges with accumulated flow; the insight is that stress is an electrical flow problem — the assignment should make omega nearly orthogonal to the slow Fiedler-like eigenvectors, i.e., balance the frequency 'charge' across the graph's spectral cuts.

innovation_hook: The announced score is a strategic instrument, not a preference report: optimal announcements deliberately misstate your own weights, bending them against the printed cost curvature to squeeze bids.
why_it_generalizes: The gap between the metric you announce and the outcome you want is the deepest incentive lesson (Goodhart by construction); designing the announcement as a lever transfers to KPIs, leaderboards, and RFPs.
seed_example: Input: 60 suppliers with printed cost curves c_j(q); each bids the (quality, price) pair maximizing its own profit subject to winning under your announced scoring function. Output: a scoring function from a 12-parameter family (weights, caps, kinks). verify.py computes every supplier's closed-form best response, selects the winner, and scores the buyer's TRUE utility V(q) - p, where V is printed and differs from anything you announce. Trap: announcing your true utility as the score invites cheap quality inflation wherever cost curves are flat -- truthful announcement is strictly dominated.

innovation_hook: Global reaction-diffusion parameters only set an average texture, so hitting a specific arrangement requires spatially modulating the parameters as a field.
why_it_generalizes: Teaches controlling an emergent pattern by shaping the parameter landscape, a control-of-self-organization skill.

innovation_hook: Because domains share morphology, spending dictionary slots on stems (which compose with every domain's affixes under the greedy parser) strictly dominates memorizing surface strings — a factorization the pooled frequency count never surfaces.
why_it_generalizes: Building one vocabulary/basis that serves heterogeneous distributions under a frozen consumer algorithm is the tokenizer/codebook problem in miniature.
seed_example: gen.py emits K=6 corpora (~200KB each) from different synthetic 'domains' built from shared hidden stems plus domain-specific affixes; the solver outputs a dictionary of D=512 substrings; verify.py compresses each corpus with a FIXED greedy longest-match parser and scores the MINIMUM byte-savings ratio over corpora. Trap: mining frequent substrings from the concatenation memorizes whole domain-specific words and starves the smallest domain.

innovation_hook: Replication changes the problem from partitioning to set-cover shaping — the biggest wins come from making covers cheap, not from putting friends together.
why_it_generalizes: Denormalization-vs-locality tradeoffs under a duplication budget recur in columnar stores, feature stores, and CDN object bundling.
seed_example: Input: a record schema of 64 fields with byte widths, 1e4 fixed queries each touching a field subset with a row-count weight, and a replication budget allowing up to r field copies. Solver outputs a partition of fields into segments plus the replicas; checker charges each query the summed page counts (ceil of segment byte width times rows over page size) of the minimal segment set covering its fields, choosing the cheapest cover deterministically. Trap: clustering by pairwise co-access affinity misses that replicating one narrow hot field into two segments collapses whole families of covers, and that a rarely-queried wide field must be quarantined even against its affinity; exact vertical partitioning is NP-hard so structure mining plus local search is required.

innovation_hook: The plant's true state is contaminant concentration, not inventory — sometimes producing at a loss buys future capacity, a claim no myopic margin calculation supports.
why_it_generalizes: Slow poison-state dynamics with cheap-now/expensive-later feedback (tech debt, soil salinity, model drift) all require the same shadow-price reformulation.
seed_example: Input: daily scrap offers (volume, contaminant ppm, price), limited virgin metal, a hard ppm cap on melt output, and a fixed fraction of each day's output returning as internal scrap next period, concentrating contaminants. Solver outputs a daily buy/blend/produce plan; verify.py tracks the exact contaminant mass balance and scores profit. Trap: maximizing daily recycled fraction poisons the internal loop weeks later; strong solutions treat contaminant concentration as a slow state with a shadow price and run planned deep-dilution purge cycles even at a daily loss.

innovation_hook: Design in the seam-interface language first (boundary types as a constraint graph), then enumerate block interiors within each interface class using dihedral canonical forms to certify distinctness cheaply.
why_it_generalizes: Trains interface-first decomposition with quotient-space deduplication — a modular-design skill far beyond quilting.
seed_example: Input: B x B blocks of b x b half-square triangles, c colors, per-color global count tolerance, a diagonal-continuity spec. Solver outputs the full quilt; checker verifies all B^2 blocks are pairwise inequivalent under the dihedral group, color counts are within tolerance, and scores block count + total length of diagonal seam lines continuing across block borders. Trap: generating distinct blocks independently almost surely breaks seam continuity, while enforcing continuity by copying neighbors makes blocks dihedrally equivalent — the constraints pull in opposite directions.

innovation_hook: A mirror's value is an integral over sun positions minus interference with neighbors, so the marginal mirror belongs at the shading frontier rather than the geometric sweet spot.
why_it_generalizes: Trains optimizing placements whose values are coupled through occlusion — a submodular-interference structure common to sensors, antennas, and coverage design.
seed_example: gen.py emits a ground grid, a tower position and height, a discrete sun path (list of directions across the day), and a mirror budget; the solver outputs mirror positions. verify.py traces integer rays for each sun step with exact shading and blocking tests between mirrors and scores total energy delivered over the day. Trap: hexagonal densest packing near the tower maximizes noon output but self-shades catastrophically at low sun angles; optimal density decays with radius and is azimuthally asymmetric, tracking where the sun path actually spends time.

innovation_hook: The real leverage is in the discrete recoloring degrees of freedom that reshape the transition graph itself, not in touring the graph better.
why_it_generalizes: Choosing item variants to make a sequence of asymmetric switch costs collapse is the structure of setup-dependent lot scheduling, codec switching, and context-switch minimization.
seed_example: Input: L layers, each a set of regions with a required color class (each class lists 2-3 acceptable concrete colors), and an asymmetric purge matrix P[c1][c2] (dark-to-light costs far more than light-to-dark). Solver outputs, per layer, a concrete color per region and a printing order of regions; verify.py charges P for every transition including the carryover from each layer's last color to the next layer's first, and scores total purge volume. Trap: per-layer greedy TSP over transitions looks optimal layer-by-layer but ignores carryover chaining and the recoloring freedom — the strong solution picks concrete colors globally so consecutive layers end and begin in the same color, effectively making the expensive dark-to-light purge happen once per print instead of once per layer.

innovation_hook: Balanced words with maximal factor complexity are the Sturmian/rich family, not random or periodic strings.
why_it_generalizes: Finding the object that jointly optimizes two opposed criteria is a broadly reusable insight.
seed_example: Input: alphabet size a, a length window K, a per-letter balance/frequency constraint, and target length L, seeded. The solver outputs a word; the checker, only if the balance constraint holds, counts distinct factors of each length l<=K and scores their sum. Trap: a random word maximizes factor counts but violates the balance constraint (score zero), while a periodic balanced word has tiny factor complexity. The insight is that Sturmian/rich words hit near-maximal factor complexity while staying perfectly balanced, occupying the exact sweet spot.

innovation_hook: Worst-case-over-loads rewards members lying in the intersection of many load paths — a shared-subspace insight that per-case optimization plus union never discovers within budget.
why_it_generalizes: Finding single components that simultaneously serve many stress scenarios is the physical version of finding shared structure across tasks.
seed_example: Input: a node grid with supports, a total member-length budget, and 16 published load cases (gravity, four wind directions, moving point loads). Solver outputs the bar set with cross-section choices; verify.py solves deterministic linear elastic equilibrium per case (fixed dense solver, fixed ordering) and scores the minimum over cases of (yield stress minus max member stress), zero if any case is unstable. Trap: optimizing the dominant gravity case yields a frame with no lateral stiffness that scores zero on the wind cases; the ceiling finds diagonals that serve as load paths for several sweep members simultaneously instead of adding per-case bracing.

innovation_hook: Majority-dynamics tipping is achieved by nucleating self-sustaining local-majority domains, favoring dense pockets over global hubs.
why_it_generalizes: Zealot-driven tipping models norm change, standard adoption, and controllability of consensus systems.
seed_example: Input: a social graph, an initial opinion per node, and a target opinion. Solver picks a minimum set of stubborn 'zealot' nodes fixed to the target; verify.py runs deterministic synchronous majority dynamics to a fixed point and passes only if the whole network converges to the target, scoring the number of zealots used (minimize). Trap: placing zealots on the highest-degree hubs is wasteful because a hub gets out-voted by its own many neighbors; tipping needs zealots that dominate small local majorities and nucleate self-sustaining domains.

innovation_hook: Switching can beat averaging: because the factors need not commute, each phase only has to kill the other phase's slow subspace, a genuinely dynamical design criterion with no static analogue.
why_it_generalizes: Designing alternating operators whose weaknesses are mutually orthogonal is the core of splitting methods, alternating projections, and curriculum scheduling.
seed_example: Input: a graph whose edge set must be partitioned into a 'day' set A and 'night' set B (each edge used in exactly one shift), with per-shift edge-count caps; step time tau fixed. Output: the partition. verify.py forms exp(-tau L_A) exp(-tau L_B) restricted to the disagreement subspace and scores its spectral norm (the Floquet contraction factor per period). Trap: splitting edges to make each shift a balanced 'half graph' optimizes the average Laplacian, but the product of two exponentials is governed by non-commuting geometry — two complementary spanning trees whose slow eigenvectors are mutually misaligned contract strictly faster than any near-equal static split.

innovation_hook: Subsidy only buys accuracy where budget constraints truncate disagreeing trades -- replaying the printed sequences makes the marginal value of beta exactly computable, turning allocation into resource optimization with planted non-concave pockets.
why_it_generalizes: Allocating an incentive budget across sub-markets by simulating frozen participant behavior is a template for grants, bounties, and liquidity mining; it rewards building an internal replay model of the evaluator.
seed_example: Input: 100 binary questions, each with a printed ordered sequence of traders (belief, budget) and the true outcome. Output: LMSR liquidity beta_q per question with sum <= B. verify.py replays each sequence in closed form (each trader moves price toward belief until budget or belief binds) and scores summed log-score accuracy of final prices minus lambda times realized subsidy payout. Trap: uniform liquidity idles subsidy on questions where the trader sequence already agrees, and 'more liquidity where more traders' fails because deep-pocketed agreeing crowds need none -- the planted high-disagreement questions carry all the marginal accuracy.

innovation_hook: The machine's limit cycle is a resource: schedule to intercept the oscillation instead of forcing setpoints, turning waiting time into free control.

Trap: FIFO service or batching-by-type fights the thermal oscillation; the insight is temperature surfing, sequencing drinks to intercept the trajectory as it crosses each window and using flushes as free downward control.

innovation_hook: Because releases cannot be recalled and travel with lag, you must pre-empt future inflow spikes and deficits rather than react period by period.
why_it_generalizes: Irreversible flow control against a lagged deterministic forecast is the structure of inventory, buffering, and cascade control problems.
seed_example: Input: a chain of reservoirs with capacities, a deterministic inflow schedule per period, downstream travel lags, and demand nodes with flood (overflow) and drought (deficit) penalties. Each period the solver outputs releases per reservoir (releases are irreversible; water routes downstream with lag). The checker simulates all periods and reports total penalty. Trap: releasing only to meet the current period's demand overflows when a forecast inflow spike arrives with no headroom; the optimum pre-releases several periods ahead to make room and pre-fills before a drought, accounting for the routing lag so water arrives exactly when demanded.

innovation_hook: Cooperative binding lets a logarithmic tile set count out a shape that per-cell tiling wastes linearly.

The solver outputs an abstract Tile Assembly Model system (tile types with glue labels and strengths, temperature 2); the checker deterministically simulates cooperative assembly from the seed, verifies the terminal assembly equals the target, and scores tile-type count. Trap: using one unique tile per target position is always valid but uses O(n) tile types. innovation_hook: With an empty full intersection the answer is a max-weight subset chosen by a product-automaton DP, not greedy priority.

innovation_hook: The objective is a graph-embedding of the transition matrix into tree metric space, and Huffman is the trap baseline that provably ignores every off-diagonal term.

innovation_hook: Cancellation must be computed in the time-varying stiffness metric, not in geometry — equal and opposite is unequal once the structure has hardened.
why_it_generalizes: Error-canceling sequencing under a drifting gain models annealing schedules, feedback trimming in analog calibration, and rebalancing under market impact.
seed_example: Input: a planar frame graph with nominal node coordinates; each edge must be welded from one chosen side, pulling both endpoints by delta/(1 + stiffness), where stiffness of a node = number of already-welded edges in its component, and the pull direction is the edge normal signed by the chosen side. Solver outputs a weld order with side choices; verify.py accumulates displacements deterministically and scores the maximum final node deviation from nominal. Trap: the intuitive symmetric alternation (weld opposite sides in mirror pairs) fails because stiffness grows during the sequence, so the second pull of a 'canceling' pair is weaker than the first; true cancellation requires pairing welds across time with matched stiffness states.

innovation_hook: Robustness here means combinatorial avoidance — keeping every long dependency chain transversal to every perturbation subset — which no amount of slack-padding on the nominal critical path achieves.

Trap: adding slack along the nominal critical path fails because perturbations hit whole subsystems, creating new critical paths through them; the ceiling routes the schedule so no perturbation subset contains a long chain — an anti-alignment of chains with the published subsets.

innovation_hook: Detection power is the leading eigenvalue of a DtN perturbation operator, and the min-over-family score turns probe design into a spectral covering problem — neither intuition is visible from the circuit diagram.

seed_example: Input: a resistor mesh with known pristine conductances and a finite seeded family of possible hidden defects (conductance drops in small regions); a budget of q boundary current patterns with bounded integer entries. Trap: one strong dipole between distant boundary nodes sends current through the bulk and sees nothing from off-axis defects; because the score is a min over the family, patterns must both focus current through candidate regions (approximating leading eigenfunctions of the DtN difference operator) and diversify to cover the whole family.

Trap: greedy per-slot letter choice deadlocks at the center slot, which must simultaneously be near a letter and near a letter when rotated — near-fixed-points of the rotation are rare and must be planned for.

innovation_hook: Each irreversible merge must be judged by the final K-cluster partition it commits you toward, not by the nearest available pair.
why_it_generalizes: Bottom-up irreversible grouping with a global objective models hierarchical clustering, coalition formation, and incremental consolidation.
seed_example: Input: a set of points with pairwise distances and a target number of clusters K. The solver outputs an ordered sequence of pairwise cluster merges (a dendrogram); each merge is permanent and adds a linkage cost that depends on the current shapes of the two clusters. The checker cuts the dendrogram at K clusters and scores a modularity-like partition quality minus accumulated linkage cost. Trap: greedy nearest-pair (single-linkage) merging chains points into a stringy cluster that wrecks the final modularity, and the merge is irreversible; you must anticipate the K-cluster cut while merging, sometimes accepting a costlier early merge to avoid a bad global partition.

innovation_hook: Perfect scores correspond to complete mappings/graceful sequencings, whose existence is controlled by the Sylow-2 subgroup (Hall-Paige), so the solver must detect which regime G is in and either construct via coset-ladder recursion or provably bank the achievable maximum minus the parity defect.
why_it_generalizes: Recognizing a parity-type algebraic obstruction and optimizing to the true ceiling rather than fighting it teaches ceiling-awareness in constructive search.
seed_example: gen.py gives an abelian group G = Z_{n1} x Z_{n2} (orders chosen across sequenceable and non-sequenceable cases); solver prints an ordering g_1..g_{|G|} of all elements; verify.py counts distinct partial sums plus distinct consecutive differences (maximize, ceiling 2|G|-1). Trap: local search on swaps stalls around 85-90% because the last collisions are governed by a global parity/Sylow obstruction, not by local moves.

innovation_hook: Bounded-palindrome words at scale come from morphic constructions, not incremental greedy avoidance.
why_it_generalizes: Reaching for an infinite-family construction when greedy dead-ends is a broadly useful reflex.

The solver outputs a word; the checker verifies no palindromic factor longer than p occurs and scores the length attained before the first violation (L if clean). Trap: greedy letter-by-letter avoidance backtracks into dead ends far short of L. The insight is that morphic constructions produce arbitrarily long words over small alphabets whose palindromes stay bounded, and the morphism can be tuned to the given bound p.

innovation_hook: A minimum-two-cover bonus on critical nodes breaks the submodularity of coverage, so redundancy and reach must be co-optimized rather than covered greedily.
why_it_generalizes: Saturating-plus-redundancy objectives arise in monitoring, backup, and fault-tolerant coverage broadly.
seed_example: Input: a graph with per-node importance and candidate sensor sites whose coverage decays with distance; a node's covered value saturates concavely as more sensors reach it, and critical nodes yield a bonus only when reached by at least two sensors. Solver places a budgeted set of sensors; verify.py sums the saturating covered value plus double-cover bonuses (maximize). Trap: greedy diminishing-returns coverage under-protects the critical nodes because it never pays for the second sensor, while the double-cover requirement makes those nodes' value locally increasing.

innovation_hook: With replayed homogeneous responders the tariff designer is secretly scheduling the battery fleet: near-flat prices with tiny per-hour epsilon dithering desynchronize charging and beat every bold price signal.
why_it_generalizes: Signal-induced synchronization (thundering herds from retry hints, sale timers, cache TTLs) is a universal failure of naive incentives; the seed scores the herd effect exactly and rewards desynchronization-by-design.
seed_example: Input: 20k households with printed load profiles and battery specs, all running the SAME published greedy arbitrage algorithm against your tariff. Output: 24 hourly prices satisfying a revenue-neutrality check. verify.py replays every household's arbitrage, sums grid load, and scores peak load plus quadratic generation cost. Trap: any bold cheap valley makes every battery charge in the same hour -- the homogeneous best response synchronizes the fleet and manufactures a new peak worse than the original; textbook peak/off-peak spreads reliably lose to doing nothing.

innovation_hook: Growth has a built-in roughening instability, so achieving a target profile requires pre-compensating for shadowing rather than depositing to shape.
why_it_generalizes: Trains counteracting an intrinsic instability through anticipatory control, common in fabrication and feedback systems.
seed_example: gen.py emits a target surface relief profile over a 1D substrate. Solver outputs an incoming-flux schedule and a deposition mask over positions and time; verify.py runs deterministic ballistic deposition with lateral sticking and scores squared error between the grown surface and the target relief. Trap: uniform deposition undergoes KPZ roughening so the surface self-roughens away from any flat or target profile, and the ceiling anticipates shadowing (taller columns capture more flux) and pre-compensates the mask.

innovation_hook: Decompose the digraph ensemble into a consensus core (optimize hard) plus a conflict residual (compromise deliberately) — an explicit shared-versus-idiosyncratic decomposition that pooled statistics erase.
why_it_generalizes: Interface/encoding design for heterogeneous usage profiles recurs in codec tables, instruction encodings, and UI design; the consensus/residual split is the reusable idea.
seed_example: gen.py emits a key-grid geometry and K=4 corpora scenarios with digraph frequency tables (synthetic languages sharing a hidden common sub-alphabet but with conflicting top digraphs); the solver assigns 30 symbols to slots; verify.py computes finger-travel cost per scenario, divides each by that scenario's precomputed random-layout baseline, and scores the MAXIMUM normalized cost. Trap: optimizing pooled digraph frequencies picks a layout wedged between two languages' conflicting demands, worse for the max than a layout that nails the shared sub-alphabet and splits the difference only on the conflict set.

innovation_hook: Admission, not eviction, carries most of the optimality gap — refusing to cache is a decision textbook policies do not even represent.
why_it_generalizes: Learning that the null action (do nothing, keep nothing) can dominate teaches solvers to question the action space itself.
seed_example: Input: a fixed trace of 3e5 requests to objects with sizes 1B-1MB against a 4MB cache; misses cost the object's size in bytes fetched. Solver outputs, per request, an admit/bypass bit and, on admission, which resident objects to evict; checker replays, validates capacity, and sums fetched bytes. Trap: size-aware LRU variants (GDSF etc.) still admit the planted one-hit wonders and evict loop residents; with variable sizes the offline problem is NP-hard (generalized caching), and the decisive move is a whole-trace pass labeling each request by its next-use distance and lifetime value density before any eviction logic runs.

innovation_hook: A single coupling constraint is best handled by pricing it into the objective, turning one hard joint problem into many easy independent ones.
why_it_generalizes: Dual decomposition is the master pattern for separating almost-independent subsystems.
seed_example: Input = a network with per-edge capacities plus a global coupling budget linking otherwise-separate commodities (planted so the coupling is the only hard part). The solver outputs integer flows for all commodities; verify.py checks feasibility and scores total cost. Trap: solving commodities independently violates the coupling and patching afterward is costly; introducing a Lagrange multiplier (price) on the coupling constraint decomposes it into independent min-cost-flow subproblems, and a few subgradient price updates converge near the coupled optimum.

innovation_hook: Error components decay at rates tied to their spatial frequency, so the cheap plan spends ops at the scale of the error — a fact only visible by measuring per-op residual reduction.
why_it_generalizes: Trains discovering hierarchical algorithms from cost accounting alone, rewarding invention of multiscale structure rather than recalling it by name.
seed_example: gen.py emits an initial integer height disturbance on a 256x256 grid and a target maximum residual; the solver outputs a straight-line plan of relaxation ops, each naming a cell block and sweep type with cost equal to cells touched. counter.py executes the plan on the deterministic simulator, verifies the residual threshold is met, and scores total op count (minimize). Trap: uniform full-grid Jacobi sweeps need enormous op counts because smooth error decays slowly; plans that first build coarse block-averaged corrections and then refine — multigrid rediscovered as a straight-line plan — meet the threshold in a small fraction of the ops.

innovation_hook: Support voxels must be placed for the future part voxels they will enable, since a layer once built and its overhangs unsupported cannot be fixed.
why_it_generalizes: Monotone bottom-up construction with support-enabling foresight models additive manufacturing, scaffolding, and staged infrastructure.
seed_example: Input: a target 3D voxel shape on a grid. The solver outputs, for each bottom-up layer, which voxels to deposit as part material and which as (removable-cost) support; a part voxel may only be placed if the cell directly below is filled (part or support). Layers are built monotonically upward and never revised. The checker verifies buildability, that the part voxels equal the target, and reports total material plus support cost. Trap: printing only the target voxels fails wherever the shape overhangs empty space; naively supporting every overhang from the ground is expensive — the optimum anticipates where a small support column now enables many later part voxels, minimizing total support committed layer by layer.

innovation_hook: Energy is lower-bounded by the energy of A's image in every quotient Z_m -> Z_d, so an optimal set must be simultaneously spread across all quotients — building A by lifting a Sidon set through the subgroup chain controls every projection at once, which no flat search does.
why_it_generalizes: Optimizing an objective against all quotient projections simultaneously is a multiscale-invariant technique applicable to any group-indexed design.
seed_example: gen.py picks m with a rich subgroup lattice (e.g. m=2^a*3^b) and a size k; solver prints a k-subset A of Z_m; verify.py counts additive energy E(A)=#{(a,b,c,d): a+b=c+d mod m} exactly (minimize). Trap: random sets look decent but sit a constant factor above optimal, and greedy element-swaps drift into subgroup-concentrated configurations that quietly inflate energy through the quotient map.

innovation_hook: Throughput is concave in hop count via log-rate yet degraded by interference from extra relays, so an interior relay spacing beats greedy shortest-hop chaining.
why_it_generalizes: The hop-count-versus-interference balance generalizes to mesh, sensor, and any multi-hop capacity design.
seed_example: Input: source/destination pairs in the plane, a path-loss exponent, noise, and a budget of relay nodes. Solver places relays and picks each pair's multi-hop route; verify.py sets per-hop rate = log(1+SINR) with cumulative co-hop interference and reports the minimum end-to-end throughput across pairs (maximize). Trap: chaining relays to minimize hop distance backfires because the concave log-rate rewards fewer stronger hops while added relays interfere, so min-distance chaining both wastes relays and raises interference.

innovation_hook: The inverse problem decouples in frequency-of-the-profile space: perturbation components of a given spatial period talk almost exclusively to the mode they are resonant with, turning hopeless coupling into a designed basis.
why_it_generalizes: Selective control of coupled global observables via resonant perturbation components is a scientist's precision tool (spectroscopy, metamaterials, curriculum shaping).
seed_example: Input: a discrete string of n beads (fixed tension), a total mass budget distributable in integer units across beads, and target frequency ratios for the first r modes (e.g., 1 : 5/4 : 3/2). Output: mass per bead. verify.py solves the generalized eigenproblem exactly and scores log-error of achieved ratios versus targets. Trap: tuning modes one at a time fails because all modes share the same density profile; the insight is scale separation — a smooth mass trend steers low modes while an oscillatory component with period matched to a target mode's wavelength shifts that mode selectively (Bragg-like), enabling near-independent control.

innovation_hook: The exact op-count objective converts graph-structure discovery into direct score: you cannot order well without first seeing the hidden hierarchy.

innovation_hook: Slack placement must anticipate the cascade dynamics of the makeup rule: free slots are only useful if they are COMMON to the pairs likely to be cancelled together, making it a covering problem over scenario-correlated slot sets.
why_it_generalizes: Calendars and pipelines repaired by fixed rebooking rules under disruption ensembles appear in sports, manufacturing, and CI scheduling.
seed_example: gen.py emits 16 teams, venue calendars, and K=10 weather scenarios each cancelling a fixed set of (venue, date) slots; the solver outputs a full double round-robin schedule; verify.py applies the FIXED makeup rule (postponed game moves to both teams' next common free legal slot) per scenario and scores max scenario of (season-end delay + rest-fairness penalty). Trap: the most compact schedule has zero common free slots, so one cancellation cascades postponements to the season's end.

innovation_hook: A cumulative budget makes emissions a storage system run in reverse — retrofit ordering is dispatch of a depleting resource against a demand calendar, not a static ranking.
why_it_generalizes: Sequencing self-improvements that temporarily reduce capability under a hard cumulative constraint mirrors refactoring under SLA, fleet maintenance, and migration planning.

Solver outputs retrofit start times plus per-step dispatch; verify.py checks coverage every step and the running emissions integral, scoring total energy served. Trap: retrofitting dirtiest-first maximizes long-run cleanliness but creates a mid-horizon coverage crunch that forces dirty peakers on and blows the budget; the binding object is the emissions PATH, so sequencing must interleave capacity gains with demand seasons.

innovation_hook: Each firing's usefulness is a diffusion kernel evaluated at (distance, T - start), so scheduling reduces to covering targets with kernel humps — a convolutional view the per-zone reflex misses.
why_it_generalizes: Trains kernel/impulse-response thinking about actions under delay and a hard rendezvous constraint, applicable to logistics, caching, and control.
seed_example: gen.py emits a kiln cross-section grid, target cells that must all exceed temperature H at exactly step T, and burner sites; the solver outputs a straight-line plan of (burner, start, duration) firings whose fuel cost is counted exactly. counter.py simulates deterministic diffusion, checks the deadline condition at step T precisely, and scores fuel used (minimize). Trap: assigning each target zone to its nearest burner ignores that heat diffuses laterally over T steps — a long early low-power firing of one central burner preheats everything — while the exact-time condition punishes firing too early and letting heat leak away.

innovation_hook: A circulant first row makes W W^T diagonal exactly when the row's periodic autocorrelation vanishes, converting the n^2-entry problem into choosing one length-n sign/support pattern — and quadratic-residue sign patterns kill autocorrelation by character sums, with two-circulant blocks patching the nonexistence cases.

innovation_hook: Counting up-to-rotation classes demands a necklace-canonical cover, not a plain substring cover.

innovation_hook: Because codeword commitments cannot be rebalanced later, the tree must be shaped for final frequencies rather than the frequencies seen so far.
why_it_generalizes: Irreversible structural commitment under a known future distribution models online-with-foresight coding, indexing, and namespace design.
seed_example: gen.py emits a stream of symbols arriving in a fixed deterministic order with a known full frequency schedule. As each new symbol first appears, the solver must immediately assign it a binary codeword extending the current prefix-free tree; assignments are committed and the tree can never be rebalanced. counter.py computes the total encoded bits over the whole stream. Trap: assigning short codewords to whatever is frequent so far (adaptive-Huffman style) is suboptimal because early commitments freeze the tree shape; since the full future frequency schedule is computable, the optimum shapes the tree up front to fit final frequencies.

innovation_hook: Seams are a budgeted resource for absorbing Gaussian curvature, so panel boundaries must track the intrinsic curvature distribution rather than any extrinsic symmetry.
why_it_generalizes: Budgeted discontinuities placed to absorb an intrinsic obstruction is the pattern of mesh parameterization, map projection design, and domain splitting for spectral methods.

Solver outputs a partition of triangles into panels plus a planar layout of each panel; verify.py checks each panel is developed isometrically edge-to-edge (accumulating angle defect), computes per-triangle strain = deviation between the panel's planar metric and the surface metric, verifies total inter-panel seam length <= B, and scores maximum strain. Trap: equal meridian gores (the textbook balloon answer) are optimal only for rotationally symmetric surfaces; on an asymmetric patch, strain concentrates where Gaussian curvature peaks, and the insight is spending the seam budget along high-curvature ridgelines — seams should follow the curvature field, not any regular pattern.

innovation_hook: Determinism makes the fire's future a computable geometry, converting firefighting into a minimum-cut-in-time problem — choose the isochrone whose length matches your fleet's sustainable drop rate (refill queues included).
why_it_generalizes: Racing a predictable spreading process by computing where you can still win (rather than reacting to the present) transfers to epidemic barriers, cascading-failure islanding, and flood defenses.

Trap: dropping on the currently-largest flame front chases the fire and always loses cells; the deterministic spread means future isochrones are computable, and the winning play builds a fireproof CUT along a future isochrone the planes can complete just in time.

innovation_hook: Total reconfiguration cost is dominated by how often the expensive bits flip; nest the cheap bits inside and toggle costly ones monotonically.

innovation_hook: The fixed-point constraints and the cycle constraint pull in opposite directions, and the resolution is CRT surgery: act as a Hull-Dobell full-cycle affine map on one CRT factor while a low-cost fixing gadget lives in the other, so the composition satisfies both for a handful of ops.
why_it_generalizes: Splitting incompatible global constraints across CRT components is a genuinely reusable design pattern for building functions with prescribed dynamics cheaply.
seed_example: Input: composite n (10^4-10^6); the submission is a straight-line program (add/mul by constants, mod n) defining f: Z_n -> Z_n; counter.py verifies f is a permutation and that the cycle containing 0 has length >= L (a large given fraction of n), by exact iteration/cycle detection, then scores the arithmetic op count (minimize). Trap: high-degree permutation polynomials easily give long cycles but cost many ops; the 1-op map x+1 has full cycle — so gen.py forbids pure translations by requiring f to also fix a given small set pointwise, killing all cheap affine maps.

innovation_hook: The automaton's state sharing becomes circuit subexpression sharing, defeating exponential SOP expansion.
why_it_generalizes: Reuse of intermediate computation is the central efficiency insight across synthesis and compilation.

innovation_hook: The objective couples spectral progress to a self-inflicted sparsity tax — optimal schedules deliberately take smaller pivots to preserve structure, inverting the textbook pivot rule.

innovation_hook: Extrapolation is only correct if you recover the compact generating law, so flexible interpolators that ace the training regime are actively misleading.
why_it_generalizes: This is the core scientist skill: preferring an interpretable mechanism over a high-capacity fit precisely because it extrapolates.
seed_example: gen.py emits time-series descriptors (size, aspect ratio, tip count) of a simulated organism grown under several values of a control parameter, but ONLY within a narrow interior range; the held-out split is the extreme regime. Solver outputs a closed-form growth law / small ODE relating descriptors to the parameter and time; verify.py scores prediction error on the held-out extreme regime. Trap: a flexible polynomial or spline fits the interior training regime near-perfectly yet diverges under extrapolation, and the true generator is a compact allometric/saturating law, so only recovering the mechanism generalizes.

innovation_hook: The instances are only hard in the wrong representation: detecting hidden chordality converts an NP-hard-looking problem into an easy one, and the score gap exposes who noticed.
why_it_generalizes: Checking whether a problem instance secretly belongs to a tractable class before deploying heavy heuristics is a high-value transferable reflex.
seed_example: The submission is a register-allocator program; evaluator.py runs it over a frozen seeded distribution of interference instances with k registers, and a deterministic simulator counts exact spill operations for the produced allocation. Each instance is secretly an interval graph — live ranges on a line — whose variable order was scrambled; some instances need a few unavoidable spills placed well. Trap: Chaitin-Briggs-style graph coloring (the standard recipe) spills on perfectly colorable instances; recovering the interval order (e.g., via a Lex-BFS-style recognition step) makes optimal or near-optimal allocation polynomial-time.

innovation_hook: Test kernel hypotheses by their invariants under history change (constant phase lag) rather than by fit residuals on one history class.
why_it_generalizes: Kernel/impulse-response identification with structure-driven extrapolation covers rheology, battery aging, and long-memory time series.
seed_example: gen.py provides stress responses sigma(t) of a linear viscoelastic material to a few STEP strain histories, where the hidden relaxation kernel is a power law t^{-alpha} (fractional element), observed over 2 decades of time; the solver prints the kernel. verify.py scores predicted stress under held-out OSCILLATORY and ramp strain histories evaluated by exact convolution, plus parsimony. Trap: a sum of 2-3 exponentials (the textbook Maxwell fit) matches the step data over the observed decades but gets the oscillatory phase lag wrong at held-out frequencies, because only a true power law gives frequency-independent loss tangent; linearity means the step response ALONE determines all other histories via superposition — if the kernel form is right.

innovation_hook: With peaked nonlinear dynamics, the budget buys timing rather than level, so the schedule is an exercise in phase-locking oscillators to a calendar.
why_it_generalizes: Phase-aligning controllable growth curves to deadlines models cell culture, crop staging, and model training runs; it rewards thinking in trajectories and peaks instead of averages.
seed_example: gen.py emits T starter tanks with logistic vitality dynamics (feeding boosts vitality then crashes it if overfed; unfed cultures decay), a total flour budget, and dated orders each requiring some tank above a vitality threshold at bake time. Output: a timed feed/bake schedule; verify.py simulates the dynamics and sums the value of fulfilled orders. Trap: feeding all tanks evenly keeps everything mediocre and misses every threshold; the insight is phase alignment, deliberately starving tanks so their vitality peaks land on the order calendar, accepting sacrificial tanks that never bake.

innovation_hook: Crowding makes marginal (not absolute) lift the right currency, so the reformulation is a flow-over-time assignment of gliders to a drifting thermal timetable — treating thermals as machines with concave capacity to be time-shared, not destinations.
why_it_generalizes: Allocating agents to concave shared resources by marginal value under a survival budget generalizes to foraging models, spot-market bidding, and congestion-aware load balancing.
seed_example: Input: a 2D map with thermals that drift on seeded linear tracks and provide lift L/(1+n^2) per glider when n gliders circle inside; G gliders start at altitude A, sink at a fixed rate while cruising, gain altitude only in thermals, and score by reaching distant goal beacons (value per beacon) before landing out (altitude 0). Output: per glider, a timed 2D path. Checker replays altitude exactly and sums beacon values reached. Trap: all gliders diverting to the strongest thermal crowds it to near-zero lift and lands half the fleet; the crowding law is planted so the fleet optimum staggers arrivals (thermal as a time-shared pump) and routes some gliders through weak thermals nobody else wants.

innovation_hook: Prediction quality is scored through a data-structure cost (finger distance with paid moves), forcing the discovered law to be accurate specifically in the metric that matters and stable enough to amortize relocations.

seed_example: Input: the first 30k lookups of a sorted-array workload whose target positions follow a hidden drift law (e.g., seasonal sinusoid plus expansion term in the key domain); submission is an interpretable program that, before each future lookup, outputs a finger position (relocations beyond 1 per 100 steps cost 50 each). verify.py replays a held-out later window — where the expansion term has grown beyond the observed range — charging log2 of the distance from finger to target plus relocation fees. Solver outputs a daily feed recipe; the checker evaluates the surface deterministically and scores total gas. Trap: daily myopic best-mix thrashes the adaptation state — consistency beats point-optimal recipes, while spoilage forces planned deviations that must be pre-adapted days in advance.

innovation_hook: Restoration is group inference under ambiguity: choose the largest group consistent with the evidence (Occam in the subgroup lattice) and fill fully-erased orbits from the ornament's own local statistics rather than defaults.
why_it_generalizes: Trains hypothesis-selection over a structured lattice plus principled handling of unidentifiable regions — core scientific-inference moves in a checkable setting.

Trap: naive mirror-copying fails when an entire orbit is erased and fails when several groups agree on the surviving cells but disagree on completions — misidentifying the group poisons every filled cell.

innovation_hook: Fold angles live on the rigid-origami constraint manifold and can only move along its valid degrees of freedom — parametrize by those instead of per-crease.
why_it_generalizes: Optimizing on a constraint manifold rather than in raw parameter space is a general inverse-design competence.
seed_example: Input: a fixed crease pattern (planar graph of creases with mountain/valley assignment fixed) and a target 3D shape given as target positions for a subset of vertices. Solver outputs a fold angle per crease. Checker folds the pattern rigidly (propagating rotations and enforcing each interior vertex's loop-closure/developability constraint) and scores distance of achieved vertex positions to target + penalty for constraint violation (tearing/overlap). Trap: setting each crease angle independently toward the target violates the per-vertex loop-closure constraints, so the sheet tears.
