innovation_hook: Confluence fixes the normal form but not the path length; shrinking the term before duplicating rules act minimizes total work.
why_it_generalizes: Strategy choice under confluence models compiler optimization and proof-search economy.
seed_example: Input = an expression tree plus a confluent rewrite system (rules). The solver outputs a specific sequence of rule applications (position + rule) that reduces the term to its normal form; verify.py applies them, checks normal form is reached, and scores the number of rewrite steps (lower better). Trap: leftmost-outermost or leftmost-innermost default strategies take many redundant steps; because the system is confluent, a strategy that rewrites shared subterms first and applies collapsing rules early — shrinking the term before duplicating rules fire — reaches normal form in far fewer steps.

innovation_hook: A cascade of thresholds is a radix sort in disguise: each basin should resolve exactly one class boundary, so the design is choosing the sequence of cut points, not the sizes per se.
why_it_generalizes: Trains mapping a physical pipeline onto a comparator/sorting-network abstraction and optimizing the induced cut sequence — algorithmic reformulation of continuous machinery.
seed_example: gen.py emits a particle mixture (seven classes with distinct deterministic settling speeds), an inflow rate, and a total basin-volume budget; the solver outputs a sequence of basins with integer depth and length each, plus which bin each basin drains to. verify.py routes the flow through the cascade — class p settles in basin b iff residence time (volume/flow, depth-dependent) exceeds that class's threshold — and scores sum over bins of value(target class captured) minus contamination penalties. Trap: ordering basins largest-first captures everything mixed together in basin one; separation requires tuning each basin's residence time to sit between adjacent class thresholds, and the shallow-wide versus deep-narrow choice trades purity against yield nonlinearly.

innovation_hook: An ordering's cost is dominated by the downstream setup states it irreversibly commits every station into, not by any single job's local metric.
why_it_generalizes: Irreversible one-directional flow with sequence-dependent carryover models manufacturing lines, batch processing, and pipeline scheduling.
seed_example: evaluator.py runs the solver's submitted sequencing policy over a seeded family of instances: jobs must be ordered onto a conveyor passing fixed stations, each station has sequence-dependent setup costs (the setup for a job depends on the job that preceded it there) and once a job passes a station it cannot return. The evaluator reports mean total makespan/defect cost. Trap: shortest-processing-time or earliest-due-date ordering ignores the sequence-dependent setup carryover and the no-return constraint — a locally cheap ordering leaves a downstream station in a costly setup state for the rest of the run; the policy must anticipate the setup state it commits every downstream station into.

innovation_hook: Every punch's effect is the orbit of the current fold group, so the program is really a chain of subgroups compressing the target set, discovered rather than assumed from visual symmetry.
why_it_generalizes: Compressing a target set as a short program over group-action primitives is the essence of Kolmogorov-style program synthesis and appears in loop-transformation and kernel-fusion search.
seed_example: Input: a target set of holes (cells) on a 2^k x 2^k sheet. Submission is a straight-line program of ops: FOLD(line) where the line must map paper onto paper, PUNCH(x,y) which pierces every layer under that point, and UNFOLD-ALL at the end; counter.py simulates layers exactly, requires the final hole set to equal the target exactly (extra or missing holes reject), and the score is the exact op count. Trap: punching each hole with zero folds always works (baseline = |target| ops), and greedy folding along the pattern's global symmetries stalls when the pattern is only approximately symmetric; the insight is partitioning the target into orbits of different fold subgroups and sequencing folds so that partial unfolds are never needed — imperfect symmetry is handled by punching 'corrections' at intermediate fold depths.

innovation_hook: The CRT index bijection (Good-Thomas) eliminates inter-stage twiddles entirely for coprime factors, and Rader's trick converts each prime-length stage into a cyclic convolution that can be re-attacked by the same machinery — multiplication count is minimized by numbertheoretic re-indexing, not by DAG-level micro-optimization.
why_it_generalizes: Re-indexing a computation through an algebraic isomorphism to delete work outright is among the most transferable ideas in algorithm design.
seed_example: gen.py fixes a composite transform length n (e.g. 3*5*7*16) and a prime field with the needed roots of unity; the submission is an arithmetic DAG (adds free or cheap, general multiplications counted) that must compute the full length-n NTT, verified exactly by applying the DAG to all standard basis vectors; score = counted multiplications (minimize). Trap: generic mixed-radix Cooley-Tukey pays a twiddle multiplication between every stage pair, landing ~2x above the reachable count.

innovation_hook: On longer strings the optimal path switches, so only a genuine tropical automaton, not an additive fit, extrapolates.
why_it_generalizes: Detecting a regime change driven by hidden min-plus structure is a transferable law-discovery skill.
seed_example: Input (E): string-to-cost samples of a hidden min-plus (tropical) weighted automaton, split into a TRAIN regime of short strings and a held-out EXTRAPOLATION regime of longer strings. The solver outputs a tropical weighted automaton (states, transition weights); the checker verifies train fit within tolerance and scores extrapolation accuracy on longer strings. Trap: fitting a purely additive per-symbol cost (a linear model) matches short strings where one path dominates but fails on longer strings when the min-cost path switches. The insight is to recover the low tropical-rank automaton whose shortest-path structure reproduces the regime switch.

innovation_hook: The extrapolation is solvable only by combining the data's edge signatures with the stated physical constraints (continuity, monotonicity, dimensional consistency) — constraint-guided extrapolation beats any pure regression.
why_it_generalizes: Fusing weak data signals with hard structural constraints to predict beyond the measured regime is the canonical scientist's move that pure curve-fitting never learns.
seed_example: gen.py provides breakdown-voltage measurements over a safe mid-range of (thickness, temperature); the hidden law switches mechanism (avalanche-like to tunneling-like) outside the sampled range, with continuity and monotonicity constraints stated in the problem. Trap: a single smooth global fit interpolates the training range perfectly and misses the switch entirely; the two mechanisms leave an asymmetric curvature signature at opposite edges of the training window, and dimensional-analysis constraints pin the switch locus that data alone cannot.

innovation_hook: The moment expansion at t->0 and the gap dominance at t->inf give two nearly orthogonal handles hidden in one weight vector — finding the structural split (cut vs interior edges) that decouples them is the discovery.
why_it_generalizes: Matching a multi-scale response curve with one shared parameter set, by identifying which parameters own which regime, mirrors calibration problems across physics and ML.
seed_example: Input: a fixed graph topology, integer edge-weight caps, and target values of the heat trace Tr exp(-t L_w) at times spanning four decades. Output: integer weights per edge. verify.py computes the exact spectrum of the weighted Laplacian and scores max log-error across the time grid. Trap: fitting timescales sequentially (short-time first, then long-time) thrashes, because short-time traces are controlled by total weight and degrees while long-time traces are controlled by lambda_2 — and the same weights set both; the planted topology gives cut edges and interior edges asymmetric influence on the two regimes, so the fit must be posed as a joint allocation exploiting that structural lever.

innovation_hook: Against published deterministic opponents, price manipulation becomes pure planning: computing safe bluff ceilings (the max bid that still loses) from opponents' rules converts the auction into adversary-free search over budget trajectories.
why_it_generalizes: Exploiting a fully specified strategic environment -- rather than assuming worst-case -- is the mechanism-lens dual of design: reading published rules for exploitable invariants transfers to any interaction with legacy automated agents.
seed_example: Submission: a bidding program with budget B over 300 sequential second-price lots; opponents are three published rule-based bidders (proportional pacer, sniper, value-capper) with per-instance printed parameters. evaluator.py replays 20 seeded instances; score = value of lots won minus payments. Trap: truthful paced bidding wins the wrong lots -- the deterministic sniper can be baited into draining budget on decoy lots you bid up and drop, but every bluff carries the exactly computable risk of winning the decoy; naive pacing never discovers that opponents' budgets are a resource you can spend.

innovation_hook: Reusing transformed sub-assemblies turns a linear build into a near-logarithmic one, i.e. an addition-chain over shapes.
why_it_generalizes: Trains finding and exploiting repeated structure to compress a construction, the same insight behind fast exponentiation and grammars.
seed_example: gen.py emits a target 3D voxel structure with exploitable self-similarity/symmetry. The submitted plan is a straight-line program over growth macros: place-unit, translate(sub,v), reflect(sub,plane), union(sub,sub), where any already-built sub-assembly can be reused. counter.py replays the plan, verifies exact reconstruction of the target, and counts operations. Trap: emitting one place-unit per occupied voxel is O(V) operations, whereas the minimum exploits repeated motifs via duplicate-and-transform (a shape addition-chain / grammar of the structure), which per-voxel placement never approaches.

innovation_hook: The evaluator rewards meta-reasoning — spending budget on diagnosis before optimization — which no single recombined recipe can match across the mixture.
why_it_generalizes: Diagnose-then-dispatch over a portfolio is how effective scientists and solvers actually operate under heterogeneous problems.

innovation_hook: Parsimony is enforced physically as an op-count objective, making 'find the law' and 'simplify the law' the same optimization instead of a fit-plus-penalty proxy.
why_it_generalizes: Compressing observed behavior into a minimal exact program is Kolmogorov-style discovery, transferring to superoptimization and formula simplification.
seed_example: gen.py publishes the complete value table of a hidden integer function F on a small domain (all inputs |x|,|y| <= 50), where F secretly factors through a compact algebraic identity (e.g., a disguised resultant/gcd-like combination); the submission is a straight-line integer program (add/sub/mul/div/mod/cmp ops in a fixed DSL). counter.py first checks EXACT agreement with hidden ground truth on held-out large inputs (|x| up to 10^6, so table lookup and interpolation are impossible), then the score is the exact operation count of the program. Trap: piecewise/table-driven or polynomial-interpolation constructions match the published table but fail the held-out exactness gate; the only route to a small op count is genuinely discovering the identity, and further savings come from algebraic rearrangement of it.

innovation_hook: Threshold dynamics plus product scoring mean the design must guarantee every scenario's trajectory stays above stress in every field — a feasibility-first, then-optimize reformulation, with water moved to the weeks where drought scenarios' soil-moisture envelopes pinch.
why_it_generalizes: Multiplicative scenario scoring with threshold dynamics teaches guarantee-then-optimize planning used in agriculture, battery health, and SLO budgeting.
seed_example: gen.py emits a seasonal water budget, F=8 fields with distinct crop response recurrences (growth is a deterministic function of soil moisture trajectory with stress thresholds), and K=7 rainfall scenarios; the solver outputs one weekly irrigation allocation per field; verify.py simulates growth per scenario and scores the PRODUCT over scenarios of total yield. Trap: allocating water proportional to average rainfall deficit lets one drought scenario cross a stress threshold on the thirstiest crop, and the product scoring makes that near-zero factor dominate everything.

innovation_hook: The windows chain into one multi-stage rearrangement problem where wing buffers act like registers — planning prop 'lifetimes' across scenes (when to spill to the wing, when to reload) beats any per-window optimal mover.
why_it_generalizes: Lookahead spill/reload planning across capacity-limited buffers is register allocation in physical costume — the same reasoning drives multi-stage manufacturing changeovers and container restowage.

Output: per blackout window, timed robot moves. Checker replays each window, verifies every scene's layout is exact when lights come up (huge penalty per wrong prop), and scores total robot-ticks used plus number of windows exceeded. Trap: solving each blackout independently strands heavy props on the far side for later scenes; the scene SEQUENCE is planted so near-ceiling plans pre-position props two scenes ahead through the wings, treating the wings as a k-buffer sorting device across windows.

innovation_hook: Query endpoints, not data density, carry the signal: the touch cost is a function of boundary-endpoint alignment, reframing partitioning as changepoint matching under a convex build penalty.
why_it_generalizes: Adaptive chunking against an observed access pattern with nonlinear per-chunk overhead is ubiquitous: shards, parquet row groups, video keyframes.
seed_example: Input: a timeline of 1e6 timestamped records and a fixed trace of 2e4 time-range queries whose endpoints cluster at planted 'event' instants at multiple scales. Solver outputs epoch boundaries partitioning the timeline into segments; checker charges each query one touch per overlapped segment plus, once, each segment's build cost size^1.3, summing both. Trap: uniform or equal-mass epochs are the reflex; aligning boundaries to the query-endpoint clusters cuts touch cost hugely, but the superlinear build cost punishes the equally reflexive fix of many tiny segments — and the multi-scale endpoint structure means a single granularity is never right, so the partition must be locally adaptive.

innovation_hook: Ranking converters by single-output efficiency is meaningless in a coupled system — only joint fuel-to-all-demands accounting reveals the cascade windows, effectively requiring the solver to invent exergy analysis.

Trap: satisfying each demand via its 'dedicated' converter (boiler for steam, electric chiller for cold) ignores cascade paths — extracting turbine steam to drive absorption chilling covers two demands with one fuel stream; instances are planted so cascade paths dominate at specific demand-ratio windows the solver must detect.

innovation_hook: Drawdowns superpose, so the marginal value of a well depends on the whole current field — the right frame is intercepting inflow at a cut, not lowering symptoms at the target.
why_it_generalizes: Trains diminishing-returns and interception reasoning under superposition, applicable wherever interventions share a common linear response.
seed_example: gen.py emits an aquifer grid with recharge boundary cells, foundation cells whose water table must drop below a target level, and a total pump-energy budget; the solver outputs well positions with integer pump rates. verify.py runs a fixed-iteration deterministic groundwater relaxation and scores total pumping energy used, with an infeasible-score if any foundation cell stays wet (minimize). Trap: placing wells greedily at the wettest foundation cells double-counts — nearby wells share drawdown by superposition so their marginal effect collapses, while the recharge boundary silently refills anything pumped near it; efficient layouts form a sparse interception line between recharge source and foundations.

innovation_hook: The two test families are Fourier-dual (additive vs multiplicative characters), so low simultaneous imbalance requires classes that are pseudorandom for both bases — mixing coset skeletons with interval shims and certifying the balance by explicit Gauss-sum bounds, an inherently hybrid construction.
why_it_generalizes: Balancing against two incompatible symmetry groups at once forces genuinely novel hybrid designs rather than importing one textbook structure.
seed_example: gen.py picks prime p and small k; solver prints a partition of F_p^* into k classes each of prescribed size; verify.py computes, for a fixed public list of multiplicative characters and additive shifts, the imbalance of each class under each test (deviation of character sums and shifted-intersection sizes from their proportional values), scoring the maximum imbalance (minimize). Trap: interval-based classes are perfectly additive-balanced but wildly character-imbalanced; subgroup-coset classes are the reverse — any one-structure answer fails half the tests.

innovation_hook: When the quadratic-form adversary is a known family rather than all of R^n, optimal sparsification concentrates on that family's spectral support — the general-purpose theorem is the trap, not the answer.
why_it_generalizes: Right-sizing guarantees to the actual test distribution (versus worst-case theory) is a pervasive engineering-science judgment call this seed makes directly scoreable.
seed_example: Input: a dense weighted graph (n<=1500) and a hard edge quota s far below effective-resistance-sampling's comfortable regime, plus a frozen set of adversarial test vectors including indicator vectors of planted near-cuts. Output: a reweighted subgraph with <= s edges. verify.py scores max over test vectors x of |x^T L_H x / x^T L_G x - 1|. Trap: textbook leverage-score sampling is optimized for all of R^n and wastes quota certifying directions the frozen test set never probes; because the test vectors are published as a family (planted near-cut indicators plus smooth vectors), the winning sparsifier reallocates its quota to exactly the probed subspace — a deliberate, targeted departure from spectral-sparsification dogma.

innovation_hook: The design problem is monetary policy for a toy economy: keeping scrip velocity high via demurrage-style taxes and targeted refills dominates any clever initial endowment, a dynamic invisible to one-round reasoning.
why_it_generalizes: Scrip systems (compute credits, karma, internal markets) all die by hoarding or inflation; scoring a full replayed circulation makes currency-dynamics reasoning a concrete optimization skill.

Trap: equal endowments plus no tax lets scrip pool at nurses with mild schedules (they win early, hoard, then stop trading), freezing the market by week 20 -- the planted disutility correlation makes hoarding equilibria the default.

innovation_hook: Long-horizon phase coherence demands deciding WHICH frequencies share an exact rational relation and which are genuinely free — a discrete model-selection problem hiding inside a spectral fit.
why_it_generalizes: Detecting exact resonances/commensurabilities among estimated quantities appears in orbital dynamics, clock networks, and signal forensics.
seed_example: gen.py provides a noisy tide-height series that is a sum of 3 sinusoids whose frequencies are locked in exact small-integer ratios (a hidden commensurability, e.g., 2:3:7 scaled by one base rate) plus one unlocked interloper; training covers a window shorter than the full super-period. verify.py scores prediction error on a held-out window several super-periods later, plus a parsimony term rewarding fewer free frequencies. Trap: independent per-peak frequency estimation from an FFT carries per-frequency errors that dephase catastrophically over long horizons, while snapping the locked triplet to exact rationals (one shared free parameter) keeps phase coherent forever — but naively snapping ALL four ruins the unlocked component.

innovation_hook: The refreeze timer makes cleared paths a LEASED resource, so the problem becomes leasing channel-time to convoys — matching convoy composition and cadence to the decay constant rather than building static infrastructure.
why_it_generalizes: Planning around resources that decay unless actively maintained (leases, cache lines, spectrum grants) and amortizing maintenance across grouped consumers is a broadly transferable systems insight.
seed_example: Input: an ice grid where B icebreakers clear cells that REFREEZE r ticks after clearing, and M cargo ships (each with a speed and a destination port) that can traverse only clear cells; a cargo ship within 1 cell behind an icebreaker or another ship moves safely, and up to c ships may follow one breaker. Output: timed paths for all breakers and ships. Checker replays refreeze timers and scores total weighted arrival time. Trap: clearing a 'highway' first then sending ships fails because it refreezes; per-ship escort is r-safe but wastes breaker time — the refreeze constant is planted so the optimum forms mixed-speed convoys sized to c, sequenced slowest-last, with breakers leapfrogging between convoy handoff points.

innovation_hook: Free-block choice is really a TLB-locality decision: reusing a just-freed hot-page block beats the textbook best-fit/lowest-address rule, tying allocator policy to a cache model most solvers treat as separate.
why_it_generalizes: Coupling allocation policy to the memory hierarchy's state (rather than to fragmentation alone) reflects how real allocators earn performance.
seed_example: Input: a fixed alloc/free trace (sizes are powers of two, buddy-eligible) over a heap of 2^24 bytes, plus interleaved accesses to live blocks through a simulated 64-entry TLB (4KB pages, exact LRU). Solver outputs, for each allocation, which free block to place it in (splits implied) and, for each free, whether to coalesce now or defer; checker validates buddy invariants and scores TLB misses plus 0.01 per split/coalesce op. Trap: standard buddy with eager coalescing is the reflex, but eager coalescing destroys warm address ranges that the trace immediately re-allocates and re-touches — deferred coalescing keeps hot pages hot, yet deferring too long fragments the heap and forces placements onto cold pages, an explicit timing tension.

innovation_hook: Capacity is a consumable that regenerates on its own clock, so routing must be scheduled against both the season's capacity curve and self-inflicted fatigue — a spacetime budget, not a path choice.
why_it_generalizes: Trains policies that manage a regenerating shared resource across time, the structure underlying maintenance windows, battery cycling, and rate limiting.
seed_example: evaluator.py holds 30 seeded daily-temperature series and a deterministic ice model (thickness per cell evolves with temperature and shore distance; a moving load stresses ice as load divided by thickness-squared, and crossings leave fatigue that decays slowly); the submitted program reads each day's ice map and outputs that day's convoy: route and cargo mass. Score = total cargo delivered over the season, zero for any day a stress limit is exceeded (maximize). Trap: always taking the shortest route fatigues one corridor until it fails in midseason exactly when ice is thickest elsewhere; strong programs rotate corridors and schedule heavy hauls for the deep-freeze window predicted from the temperature trend.

innovation_hook: Parametrizing the completion as U*V^T with r columns turns visible entries into bilinear equations over F_p solvable by alternating linearization plus finite-field closure of the solution variety — and the sparse spikes are exactly the inconsistencies of the best bilinear fit, so detection and completion are one computation.
why_it_generalizes: Low-rank-plus-sparse recovery over finite fields (no norms, exact algebra only) forces the certificate-based reasoning that numeric intuition usually hides.
seed_example: gen.py builds an m x m matrix over F_p as rank-r (r=2,3) plus a few sparse spikes, then reveals 30-50% of entries; solver prints all hidden entries; verify.py checks consistency with revealed entries and scores the rank of the completed matrix plus a spike-count penalty (minimize). Trap: filling unknowns with zeros or row-averages is consistent but yields near-full rank; local rank-driven fills stall because rank is a global algebraic property invisible to entrywise moves.

innovation_hook: Training data confound path and endpoint by design — the solver must realize the law maps FUNCTIONS to outcomes and posit branch memory to break the confound.
why_it_generalizes: Path-dependent outcome modeling (annealing, curing, training curricula) requires functional inputs and hidden branch state, a step beyond pointwise regression.
seed_example: gen.py provides final-state measurements m_final for many temperature PROTOCOLS T(t) (piecewise-linear schedules), where the hidden law is a first-order transition with metastable branches: the state follows the current branch until it loses stability at protocol-dependent spinodal points, so outcomes depend on the path, not the endpoint; training protocols are all monotone cooling. The solver prints the branch diagram (two branch curves plus jump thresholds); verify.py scores m_final predictions for held-out NON-monotone protocols (cool-reheat-cool) plus parsimony. Trap: regressing m_final on protocol endpoint or on summary features fits all monotone data (where path and endpoint are confounded) and fails on reheating protocols; only an explicit branch-plus-jump mechanism predicts which protocols get trapped in the metastable state.

innovation_hook: The value of a slot is its marginal PAIR-SEPARATION power against the currently-confusable narrative pairs of the worst family — a discrimination objective that inverts the quality-greedy intuition and rewards booking 'boring' slots that split hypotheses.
why_it_generalizes: Committing an observation schedule to disambiguate competing hypothesis classes is experiment design as scientists actually face it, with the classic signal-vs-discrimination trap.
seed_example: gen.py emits T=60 bookable observation slots (each slot sees a subset of 40 sky sectors with a quality weight) and K=6 'story families' — each family a set of candidate event narratives (sector, onset window, decay shape); the solver books B=15 slots once; verify.py counts, for each family, the fraction of narrative pairs its bookings can distinguish (deterministic separability check) and scores the MINIMUM over families. Trap: booking the highest-quality slots maximizes raw signal but leaves two families' narratives pairwise-confusable because their distinguishing sectors only appear in low-quality slots.

innovation_hook: Contamination is a one-way state ratchet, so the winning reformulation partitions the fleet into castes aligned with the grade lattice and treats rare cross-caste transitions as a batched, scheduled commodity — an assignment insight, not a routing one.
why_it_generalizes: Partitioning agents by an irreversible state lattice to avoid expensive state resets generalizes to security-clearance workflows, allergen scheduling in food plants, and taint-tracking in build systems.
seed_example: Input: a facility of zones with cleanliness grades connected by airlocks (each cycle takes t ticks, holds up to c robots, and is a shared mutex), R robots, and J jobs (zone, tick-window, duration); a robot entering a dirtier zone gets 'contaminated' and must pass a decontamination airlock cycle (slower) before re-entering any cleaner zone. Output: per robot, a timed itinerary of jobs and airlock cycles. Checker replays contamination states and airlock occupancy, scoring weighted job lateness plus airlock cycles used. Trap: nearest-robot job assignment makes every robot ping-pong across grades and the decon airlocks become the bottleneck; the planted job mix rewards CASTE assignment — dedicating robot subsets to grade bands so most jobs need no decon at all.

innovation_hook: The log is simultaneously a write-side batching problem and a read-side (recovery) index — ordering records within each sealed page by crash-relevance turns replay cost into a design variable nobody prices.
why_it_generalizes: Designing the write path for the replay path (logs consumed by recovery, streams consumed by backfills) is a real and rarely formalized systems skill.
seed_example: Input: a fixed timeline of 1e5 transaction writes (each touching known keys, with hard latency deadlines: each txn must be sealed within d steps) and a set of 100 known crash points. Submission: a straight-line plan of seal operations (each seals pending records in a chosen order into one log page, cost 20 + 1 per record) placed on the timeline; the counter verifies deadlines, then for every crash point replays the log from the last complete seal, charging one replay probe per record scanned to restore only the keys needed at that crash (records for irrelevant keys still cost their scan). Total score = seal costs + summed replay costs. Trap: sealing at fixed intervals or per-deadline is the reflex; the known crash points reward placing seals just before them, and the in-page record ORDER matters because replay scans stop once its needed keys are restored — a layout-inside-the-batch dimension invisible to pure batching analysis.
